mcqData.text:
Read the stem and answer the question: Mr. Rafiq, a garments owner, is highly conscious of his firm's production operations. Despite his busy schedule with other ventures, he visits the garment factory daily and collects detailed performance summaries from the factory manager. Q. Through his proactive oversight, what critical operational insights does he stay informed about?

(i) Total volume of units produced
(ii) Qualitative standards and value of employee outputs
(iii) Operational speed and efficiency of the workers

Which of the following is correct?
mcqData.explanation: Direct physical inspection and regular managerial reporting keep an executive informed about output quantity (i), qualitative standards (ii), and workforce speed (iii). Therefore, all options apply.

mcqData.text: Read the stem and answer the question: Mr. Azam, the owner of a garment factory, visits his plant once a month. He tours the production lines, speaks with a few workers and officials to check progress, and gives necessary instructions. Q. Mr. Azam's method of visiting the floor to check on progress matches which control technique?
mcqData.explanation: Walking the production floor, speaking with workers face-to-face, and observing real-time progress is known as Personal Observation (ব্যক্তিগত পর্যবেক্ষণ).

mcqData.text:
Read the stem and answer the question: Mr. Azam, the owner of a garment factory, visits his plant once a month. He tours the production lines, speaks with a few workers and officials to check progress, and gives necessary instructions. Q. What positive operational outcomes will occur as a result of Mr. Azam's on-site instructions?

(i) Planned production targets will be achieved successfully
(ii) Employees will become more focused and attentive toward tasks
(iii) Management can stay well-informed about floor problems

Which of the following is correct?
mcqData.explanation: Personal interaction keeps staff focused (ii), highlights operational problems early (iii), and ensures production targets are achieved (i). All points are correct.

mcqData.text: In a classic Gantt Chart used for project management scheduling, what metric or dimension do the horizontal bars represent?
mcqData.explanation: In a Gantt Chart, rows list the tasks, while the horizontal parallel lines measure time duration (সময়)—showing when each activity starts and ends.

mcqData.text: Which of the following control methods is most effective and suitable for standard mass-manufacturing production enterprises?
mcqData.explanation: Break-Even Point Analysis (সমচ্ছেদ বিন্দু বিশ্লেষণ) is ideal for mass production because it pinpoints the exact output volume where total revenue matches total expenses, defining safety margins.

mcqData.text: What action should management take if actual performance outcomes significantly exceed the established standard targets?
options: Re-evaluating and raising the standard targets
Recording the outcomes with additional weight
mcqData.explanation: When actual output beats the baseline without sacrificing safety, it shows the initial target was too low. Management should re-evaluate and raise the standard targets (আদর্শ মান পুনঃনির্ধারণ) for the next cycle.

mcqData.text: Which of the following steps represents the final task or concluding phase within the management control process?
mcqData.explanation: The control process finishes by taking corrective measures (সংশোধন মূলক ব্যবস্থা গ্রহণ). This step adjusts workflows to line up with the original plan.

mcqData.text: Read the stem and answer the question: Mr. Rimon is a manager. He decides to produce 12,000 units of products per month in his organization. However, at the end of the month, the actual production turns out to be 9,000 units. Q. What is this pre-determination of production volume by Mr. Rimon called?
mcqData.explanation: A pre-determined quantitative target set to measure and compare future actual performance is called a standard yardstick or standard (আদর্শ মান) in control theory.

mcqData.text:
Read the stem and answer the question: Mr. Rimon is a manager. He decides to produce 12,000 units of products per month in his organization. However, at the end of the month, the actual production turns out to be 9,000 units. Q. To ensure that future production aligns with the set target, what steps should be taken?

(i) Verifying and re-evaluating the target numbers
(ii) Increasing factory productivity levels
(iii) Creating an appropriate and favorable work environment

Which of the following is correct?
mcqData.explanation: To bridge the 3,000-unit gap, management can review if the target was realistic (i), boost productivity via training (ii), and optimize the shop floor environment (iii). Thus, all three points are correct.

mcqData.text:
In any business enterprise, the function of controlling is considered a/an—

(i) Continuous process across cycles
(ii) Core managerial process
(iii) Administrative social process

Which of the following is correct?
mcqData.explanation: Controlling is a continuous (i) and core managerial process (ii) used to measure targets. It is not an administrative social process (iii) like community development. Thus, statements i and ii are correct.

mcqData.text: Which of the following choices represents the final step or concluding phase in the management control process?
mcqData.explanation: The final step of the control process loop is taking corrective measures (সংশোধন মূলক ব্যবস্থা), which fixes operational gaps.

mcqData.text: In business economics and control theory, what condition defines the Break-Even Point?
mcqData.explanation: The Break-Even Point (সমচ্ছেদ বিন্দু) represents the specific volume of sales where Total Income exactly equals Total Expense ($\text{আয়} = \text{ব্যয়}$), yielding zero profit and zero loss.

mcqData.text: Following the standard sequence of management cycle functions, which task comes immediately after completing the control phase?
mcqData.explanation: Controlling completes the management cycle. The data and performance insights gathered during this phase flow directly back into the next round of Planning (পরিকল্পনা).

mcqData.text:
Within corporate management theory, which of the following choices represent core characteristics of control?

(i) Acts as the functional basis for future plans
(ii) Operates as an ongoing, continuous process
(iii) Executed purely as a task for top-level executives
mcqData.explanation: Control provides feedback data that shapes future planning (i) and functions as a continuous process (ii). It is performed across all tiers of management, not just by top-level executives (iii). Thus, statements i and ii are correct.

mcqData.text: Through which core management function are the actual operating results and performance of an organization verified?
mcqData.explanation: Controlling (নিয়ন্ত্রণ) verifies operational results by checking performance against set expectations, highlighting deviations, and correcting issues.

mcqData.text:
Which of the following tools and charts are recognized as modern management control techniques?

(i) Break-Even Point Analysis
(ii) Gantt Chart
(iii) Accident-Proneness Test
mcqData.explanation: Break-Even Point Analysis (i) and Gantt Charts (ii) are established techniques for financial and operational control. Accident-proneness tests (iii) are psychological staffing selection tools. Thus, statements i and ii are correct.

mcqData.text: Read the stem and answer the question: Omar Faruk is the production manager of a blanket manufacturing enterprise. His target annual production capacity is set at 600,000 units. Two-thirds of this total volume is manufactured during peak months from November to February, while the remaining volume is distributed equally across the other months of the year. Q. What is this total target production volume of 600,000 units called in the control process?
mcqData.explanation: A pre-determined quantitative target (600,000 units) set to evaluate and compare actual production outcomes is defined as a standard yardstick or standard (আদর্শ মান).

mcqData.text: Read the stem and answer the question: Omar Faruk is the production manager of a blanket manufacturing enterprise. His target annual production capacity is set at 600,000 units. Two-thirds of this total volume is manufactured during peak months from November to February, while the remaining volume is distributed equally across the other months of the year. Q. Based on the production breakdown, what is the exact target production volume for the month of April?
mcqData.explanation: Peak output (Nov–Feb, 4 months) = $\frac{2}{3} \times 600,000 = 400,000\text{ units}$. Remaining output = $600,000 - 400,000 = 200,000\text{ units}$. Remaining months = $12 - 4 = 8\text{ months}$. April target = $\frac{200,000}{8} = 25,000\text{ units}$ (২৫,০০০).

mcqData.text: What is considered the primary, central objective behind implementing a management control framework?
mcqData.explanation: Although control assists in setting quality benchmarks and tracking performance, its primary, cost-saving focus is minimizing operational waste and efficiency losses (অপচয় হ্রাস করা).

mcqData.text: Which specific control technique uses thick, dark horizontal bars to display the start dates, durations, and completion deadlines of project components?
mcqData.explanation: A Gantt Chart (গ্রান্ড চার্ট) uses distinct horizontal bars to graph timelines, mapping out exactly when different project phases begin and wrap up.

fullCQData.stem: Fast Safe Ltd.' is a t-shirt manufacturing company. They have a good reputation for producing high-quality t-shirts. In the current year, the company set its production target at 700,000 units. After five months, it was observed that only 200,000 units of t-shirts had been manufactured. Under such circumstances, the company increased its supervision, identified the errors and deviations, and took measures to resolve them. They hope to achieve the target within the specified time.

questions.label: a
questions.text: What is the basis of control?
questions.answer: Planning is the fundamental basis of control because the success of the control process depends entirely on comparing actual performance against the pre-established standards set during the planning phase.

questions.label: b
questions.text: Control is a continuous process' — Explain.
questions.answer:
Knowledge: Control is called a continuous process because it is an ongoing, uninterrupted managerial activity that operates throughout the entire life cycle of an organization.

Realization: Management cannot implement control just once and abandon it. As long as organizational operations exist, actual performance must be constantly measured, compared against planned standards, and corrected. As new plans are made and executed, the control cycle repeats itself to address fresh challenges. If this monitoring loop pauses even briefly, deviations will go unnoticed, leading to operational chaos and failure to achieve organizational goals.

questions.label: c
questions.text: Which initial step of control did 'Fast Safe Ltd.' follow? Explain.
questions.answer:
Knowledge (Para 1): 'Fast Safe Ltd.' initially followed the "Measurement of Actual Performance" (প্রকৃত কার্যফল পরিমাপ) and "Comparison of Performance with Standards" (মানদণ্ডের সাথে প্রকৃত কার্যফলের তুলনা) steps of the control process.

Contextual Connection (Para 2): The stem indicates that the company had a pre-established plan or standard of manufacturing 700,000 units of t-shirts for the year. To evaluate their progress, management assessed the actual workflow after a five-month interval and discovered that the factory floor had turned out only 200,000 units.

Explanation & Conclusion (Para 3): The control process systematically begins with setting standards, followed by measuring real-time output and comparing the two data points to detect any gaps. By actively calculating the exact number of t-shirts produced (200,000 units) and analyzing this data against their annual benchmark, Fast Safe Ltd. executed the core measurement and comparison steps of control, which allowed them to realize they were facing a critical production deficit.

questions.label: d
questions.text: Analyze the logical justification of the measures taken by the company under the changed circumstances.
questions.answer:
Knowledge (Para 1): The measures taken by the company—increasing supervision, identifying deviations, and implementing corrective actions—are highly justified as they represent the final and most critical stage of control, "Taking Corrective Action" (সংশোধনমূলক ব্যবস্থা গ্রহণ), which is essential for project recovery.

Contextual Analysis (Para 2): Finding themselves severely behind schedule (only 200,000 units out of 700,000 produced in five months), Fast Safe Ltd. did not abandon their targets. Instead, they adapted to the changed circumstances by tightening floor monitoring, locating the root causes of the slow production rate, and deploying administrative fixes to accelerate the manufacturing line.

Justification & Logical Arguments (Para 3): The logical necessity of taking these prompt corrective measures can be analyzed through the following points:

• Preventing Project Failure: Without increasing supervision and correcting the flaws, the production line would have continued at its slow pace, causing a massive supply deficit and a direct blow to the company's reputation.

• Maximum Resource Optimization: Identifying errors allows management to eliminate raw material waste, fix machine inefficiencies, or address worker negligence, ensuring the factory runs at maximum capacity.

• Restoring Strategic Alignment: Corrective actions bridge the gap between actual sluggish performance and the original ambitious plan, realigning the daily factory output with corporate sales commitments.

• Boosting Workplace Discipline: Tightening supervision sends a clear message to the workforce, reducing idle time and fostering a highly focused, goal-driven working environment.

Conclusion (Para 4): In conclusion, the steps taken by the management of Fast Safe Ltd. are completely logical and highly practical. Merely identifying a shortfall is useless unless it is followed by rapid administrative corrections. By turning their control data into active field intervention, the authorities have effectively synchronized their manufacturing speed with the remaining timeline, making their goal of hitting the 700,000-unit mark fully achievable.

fullCQData.stem: Aloha Ltd.' is an international standard garment exporting enterprise. They have a massive reputation in the market for manufacturing premium quality apparel. In the year 2022, the company achieved the prestigious ISO-9000 certification. At the beginning of 2023, the enterprise fixed its annual production target at 700,000 units. However, in July 2023, it was observed that only 300,000 units of garments had been manufactured. Under these circumstances, the company increased its supervision, identified the errors and deviations, and took measures to correct them. They hope to reach their goal within the specified timeframe.

questions.label: a
questions.text: What is the last function of management?
questions.answer: Control is the last or final function of the management process.

questions.label: b
questions.text: Why is control called a 'terminal' (or period-end) function? Explain.
questions.answer:
Knowledge: Control is termed a terminal or period-end function because its core operations—such as measuring output and identifying gaps—take place at the end of an operational cycle or a specified financial period after execution is complete.

Realization: Management functions operate in a sequential loop. Planning starts the cycle, and control concludes it. An administrator cannot measure actual performance or detect deviations until the operational work has progressed for a set period or reached its final deadline. Once a production cycle, quarter, or year wraps up, control steps in to analyze the final numbers against the initial blueprints. This makes it terminal in nature, turning the final performance data into lessons for the next planning phase.

questions.label: c
questions.text: Which initial step of control did 'Aloha Ltd.' follow? Explain.
questions.answer:
Knowledge (Para 1): 'Aloha Ltd.' initially followed the "Measurement of Actual Performance" (প্রকৃত কার্যফল পরিমাপ) and "Comparison of Performance with Standards" (মানদণ্ডের সাথে প্রকৃত কার্যফলের তুলনা) steps of the control process.

Contextual Connection (Para 2): The stem states that the company established a clear baseline standard: manufacturing 700,000 units of clothing for the year 2023. To evaluate their real-world progress, the management checked the factory output in July (the mid-year mark) and discovered that the production floor had turned out only 300,000 units, revealing a deficit in their expected timeline.

Explanation & Conclusion (Para 3): In administrative control, the process begins after planning with the collection of real performance data and comparing it mathematically against the target. By actively calculating the mid-year volume (300,000 units) and evaluating it against the prorated annual target, Aloha Ltd. successfully executed the measurement and comparison stages. This allowed them to recognize that they were experiencing an operational shortfall.

questions.label: d
questions.text: "The proper implementation of the latest function performed by Aloha Ltd., which corresponds to a specific step of the control process, will bring success" — Evaluate the justification of this statement in light of the stem.
questions.answer:
Knowledge (Para 1): The latest function performed by Aloha Ltd. corresponds to the final and most decisive step of the control process: "Taking Corrective Actions" (সংশোধনমূলক ব্যবস্থা গ্রহণ). I completely agree that its proper implementation is the ultimate key to their success.

Contextual Analysis (Para 2): Aloha Ltd. found itself significantly behind schedule at the mid-year mark. Merely identifying this gap would not fix the problem. To save the contract, the management immediately increased supervision, isolated the root causes of the delay, and rolled out active corrective measures to speed up the manufacturing lines.

Justification & Logical Arguments (Para 3): The argument for why the proper execution of this final step guarantees success can be analyzed through these points:

• Turning Data into Results: Standard tracking, measurement, and deviation discovery are purely analytical phases. The control loop remains useless unless management intervenes with corrective steps to change the bad data on the factory floor.

• Eliminating Root Causes: By actively finding errors, the company can fix faulty machinery, upgrade manufacturing methods, or stop worker negligence, ensuring the remaining 400,000 units are made without more delays.

• Maximizing Resource Capacity: Boosting floor supervision creates a disciplined, high-velocity work environment. This eliminates idle time and maximizes machine utility, which is essential for meeting tight deadlines.

• Preserving Corporate Reputation: For an international, ISO-9000 certified exporter, missing a delivery deadline destroys market trust. Corrective control realigns the slow factory output with their global supply commitments.

Conclusion (Para 4): In conclusion, the statement is completely justified. Identifying a 100,000-unit deficit at mid-year is simply a diagnostic warning. Success does not come from knowing you are failing; it comes from the administrative power to change that trajectory. By deploying sharp corrective actions and increasing supervision, Aloha Ltd. successfully bridges the gap between their slow actual performance and their original ambitious plan, making their goal of hitting 700,000 units highly realistic and ensuring long-term institutional success.

fullCQData.stem: Mr. 'X' is the Production Manager of Srijoni Limited. The manager of the enterprise sets a target for Mr. 'X' to manufacture 20,000 units of products within two years. Accordingly, he issues instructions to the personnel involved in production. After almost one year, during an on-site inspection, he finds that only 6,500 units of products have been manufactured. Therefore, Mr. 'X' is deeply concerned about what kind of measures can be taken to achieve the target.

questions.label: a
questions.text: What is the second step of control?
questions.answer: The second step of the control process is "Measurement of Actual Performance" (প্রকৃত কার্যফল পরিমাপ).

questions.label: b
questions.text: "The control process cannot be applied without a standard benchmark" — Explain.
questions.answer:
Knowledge: A standard benchmark serves as the foundational yardstick for control; without it, management has no baseline data to measure actual performance or identify errors.

Realization: Control is the process of checking whether organizational activities are progressing according to plan. The initial plan sets specific, measurable targets known as standard benchmarks (e.g., production volume, budget limits, or time deadlines). If there is no pre-established standard, actual performance cannot be evaluated. Management cannot determine whether a project is succeeding or failing because there is no target to compare it against. Therefore, a standard benchmark is an absolute prerequisite for control.

questions.label: c
questions.text: Calculate the amount of performance deviation and explain.
questions.answer:
Knowledge (Para 1): The amount of performance deviation in Srijoni Limited after the first year is a deficit of 3,500 units of products.

Mathematical Calculation (Para 2): To determine the exact deviation, we must calculate the prorated standard target for the first year and compare it with the actual output:

• Total standard target for 2 years = 20,000 units

• Standard target for 1 year = $\frac{20,000 \text{ units}}{2} = 10,000 \text{ units}$

• Actual performance achieved in 1 year = 6,500 units

• Performance Deviation = Actual Performance $-$ Standard Target

• Deviation = $6,500 - 10,000 = -3,500 \text{ units}$ (Deficit)

Explanation & Conclusion (Para 3): In management control, deviation represents the difference between what was planned and what was actually achieved. The mathematical analysis shows that the factory floor fell short of its mid-point milestone by 35% ($\frac{3,500}{10,000} \times 100$). This negative deviation reveals a severe bottleneck in the production line, indicating that if the factory continues at this sluggish pace, it will fail to meet the final two-year target.

questions.label: d
questions.text: What kind of measures do you think Mr. 'X' should take so that 'Srijoni Limited' can achieve its desired goal? Give your opinion.
questions.answer:
Knowledge (Para 1): To overcome this deficit and achieve the desired goal, Mr. 'X' must immediately implement "Taking Corrective Actions" (সংশোধনমূলক ব্যবস্থা গ্রহণ), which includes structural workforce reallocation, skill training, and enhanced operational supervision.

Contextual Analysis (Para 2): Facing a 3,500-unit shortfall at the end of year one, Srijoni Limited has only one year left to manufacture the remaining 13,500 units ($20,000 - 6,500$). To double their production rate from 6,500 units to 13,500 units in the remaining time, continuing with routine administrative methods will guarantee project failure.

Justification & Logical Arguments (Para 3): Mr. 'X' should implement the following specific corrective actions to salvage the corporate target:

• Investigating the Root Causes: Before deploying capital, Mr. 'X' must conduct a thorough investigation to identify why production lagged. He must check for mechanical failures, raw material supply delays, high worker absenteeism, or unrealistic baseline expectations.

• Upgrading Factory Infrastructure & Machinery: If the slow output is due to backdated equipment, the firm must invest in semi-automatic machinery or repair existing assembly lines to increase hourly output speeds.

• Deploying Extra Shifts (Overtime Operations): To handle the remaining 13,500 units, the manager must implement double shifts or incentivize current workers with overtime pay to extend active manufacturing hours.

• Tightening Floor Supervision: Increasing the frequency of supervisor monitoring loops ensures that idle time is eliminated and material waste is minimized, maximizing operational efficiency.

• Providing Quick Technical Training: If the workers lack speed due to a lack of technical skills, rapid training sessions must be conducted to optimize human labor capacity.

Conclusion (Para 4): In conclusion, I believe that while a 3,500-unit deficit is alarming, it is entirely recoverable with aggressive managerial intervention. Identifying a gap is simply a diagnostic step; the ultimate success of Srijoni Limited depends on Mr. 'X'’s administrative power to change the factory floor's trajectory. By increasing shifts, eliminating operational bottlenecks, and modernizing supervision, Mr. 'X' can turn his data into active field results, successfully hitting the 20,000-unit mark on time.

fullCQData.stem: Arun Kumar is the production manager of an enterprise. He always stays with the workers to supervise the production work. Judging by reality, he detects flaws in the ongoing control system. Although he brought the matter to the attention of the higher authority, they did not take it into account. As a result, the probability of achieving the target is very low.

questions.label: a
questions.text: What is a standard benchmark (or standard)?
questions.answer: A standard benchmark (or standard) is the pre-determined model, target, or criteria set during the planning phase against which actual performance is measured and evaluated in the control process.

questions.label: b
questions.text: What is meant by the Break-Even Point (BEP)? Explain.
questions.answer:
Knowledge: The Break-Even Point (BEP) is the specific volume of sales or production at which an enterprise's total revenue exactly equals its total costs, resulting in neither profit nor loss.

Realization: In business management, BEP serves as a vital financial control tool. Below this point, the company operates at a loss because it cannot cover its fixed overhead expenses. Above this point, the firm begins to generate a net profit. Calculating the BEP helps managers determine minimum sales targets, set safe product pricing strategies, and control operational costs to ensure the business remains financially viable.

questions.label: c
questions.text: Which control technique has Arun Kumar adopted in the stem? Explain.
questions.answer:
Knowledge (Para 1): Arun Kumar has adopted the traditional control technique known as "Personal Observation and Direct Supervision" (ব্যক্তিগত পর্যবেক্ষণ বা প্রত্যক্ষ তত্ত্বাবধান).

Contextual Connection (Para 2): The stem explicitly states that as a production manager, Arun Kumar does not manage his factory floor from an isolated office. Instead, he actively "always stays with the workers" on the shop floor to monitor their physical workflow, inspect machine operations, and guide the manufacturing process in real-time.

Explanation & Conclusion (Para 3): Personal observation is a powerful qualitative control technique where a manager relies on first-hand visual inspection rather than just reading written performance reports. By physically embedding himself in the production environment, Arun Kumar was able to directly notice immediate flaws and operational inefficiencies in the ongoing system that would otherwise look fine on paper. This hands-on tracking highlights the classic technique of direct supervisor observation.

questions.label: d
questions.text: Which principle of control was violated in the stem, causing the probability of achieving the target to be very low? Give your opinion.
questions.answer:
Knowledge (Para 1): The operational crisis occurred because the higher authorities completely violated the fundamental "Principle of Corrective Action (Promptness)" (সংশোধনমূলক ব্যবস্থা গ্রহণের নীতি) and the "Principle of Exception" (ব্যতিক্রম নীতি) in management control.

Contextual Analysis (Para 2): Arun Kumar successfully performed his duties by identifying critical system flaws on the ground and reporting them up the ladder. However, the upper management committed a fatal administrative error by completely ignoring his feedback and failing to deploy interventions. This top-level negligence broke the control loop, leaving the production line to run inefficiently and severely lowering their chances of hitting targets.

Justification & Logical Arguments (Para 3): Ignoring these core principles of control damages the organization through the following angles:

• Failure of the Control Loop: Control is completely useless without its final step—taking corrective action. Detecting a problem means nothing if management refuses to fix it; it is like diagnosing a disease but refusing to take medicine.

• Violation of Management by Exception: The principle of exception dictates that top managers should let lower managers handle routine tasks and only step in when critical, unusual deviations are reported. Since a major system flaw is an exceptional issue that ruins targets, ignoring it shows a total misunderstanding of executive control responsibilities.

• Demoralization of Field Managers: When executives dismiss the realistic, first-hand data reported by frontline managers like Arun Kumar, it destroys employee morale and discourages staff from looking out for operational problems in the future.

Conclusion (Para 4): In conclusion, the higher authority's dismissive attitude is the direct cause of the impending failure. Finding a flaw is simply a warning sign; actual corporate success comes from the administrative power to change that bad trajectory. Unless upper management shifts its approach and immediately deploys structural corrections based on Arun Kumar's floor reports, their planning blueprints will remain unachieved, leading to heavy financial and operational losses for the enterprise.

fullCQData.stem: Zoom IT' is a reputed laptop manufacturing company. In the current year, the management authority of the enterprise decided to manufacture 10,500 units of laptops. At the mid-year mark, it was observed that only 35% of the production had been achieved. As a result, the company cannot supply products according to customer demand. Due to this, the market share is shifting into the hands of competitor firms.

questions.label: a
questions.text: What is a strategic goal?
questions.answer: A strategic goal is a long-term, high-level organizational target set by top management that outlines the overarching direction and competitive positioning of the entire enterprise over a multi-year period.

questions.label: b
questions.text: "Planning is a mental process" — Explain.
questions.answer:
Knowledge: Planning is called a mental process because it involves intellectual reflection, analytical thinking, imagination, and foresight rather than physical labor.

Realization: Before executing any business activity, a manager must think ahead to determine what needs to be done, how it will be done, when it will be done, and who will do it. This requires analyzing market data, forecasting future risks, and selecting the best alternative course of action. Because it relies entirely on cognitive abilities, judgment, and brainpower to design a blueprint for the future, planning is fundamentally a creative mental process.

questions.label: c
questions.text: Calculate the amount of performance deviation of the enterprise in units.
questions.answer:
Knowledge (Para 1): The performance deviation of Zoom IT at the mid-year mark is a deficit of 1,575 units of laptops.

Mathematical Calculation (Para 2): To find the exact deviation, we must compare the prorated standard target for the mid-year mark with the actual units manufactured:

• Total standard target for the year = 10,500 units

• Mid-year standard target (50% of the year) = $10,500 \times 0.50 = 5,250 \text{ units}$

• Actual mid-year performance achieved (35% of the total) = $10,500 \times 0.35 = 3,675 \text{ units}$

• Performance Deviation = Actual Performance $-$ Mid-year Standard Target

• Deviation = $3,675 - 5,250 = -1,575 \text{ units}$ (Deficit)

Explanation & Conclusion (Para 3): In management control, deviation represents the mathematical gap between planned targets and real-world results. Zoom IT fell short of its mid-year milestone by exactly 1,575 laptops, meaning they achieved 30% less than what was expected for the mid-year timeline ($\frac{1,575}{5,250} \times 100$). This negative deviation highlights a severe bottleneck on the assembly line that has restricted their market supply capacity.

questions.label: d
questions.text: Analyze the actions to be taken by the enterprise described in the stem to reclaim its market share.
questions.answer:
Knowledge (Para 1): To reclaim its lost market share, the management of Zoom IT must immediately execute the final stage of the control loop: "Taking Rapid Corrective Actions" (সংশোধনমূলক ব্যবস্থা গ্রহণ) to ramp up production and realign with customer demand.

Contextual Analysis (Para 2): Zoom IT's current crisis is a supply-side failure, not a lack of market demand. Because of a 1,575-unit mid-year production deficit, they are failing to ship orders on time, which is forcing their loyal customer base to buy from competing brands. To stop this market bleed, they must manufacture the remaining 6,825 laptops ($10,500 - 3,675$) at double their previous operating speed.

Justification & Logical Arguments (Para 3): Management should deploy the following strategic corrective actions to recover their competitive position:

• Pinpoint and Eradicate Root Bottlenecks: Management must immediately audit the factory floor to identify the cause of the slow output—whether it is due to component supply chain delays, frequent power outages, or faulty assembly machinery.

• Introduce Double Shifts and Overtime Operations: To compensate for the lost 1,575 laptops, Zoom IT needs to maximize its factory hours by running overnight shifts or hiring temporary assembly technicians.

• Accelerate Supply Chain Logistics: If the slow output stems from a shortage of microprocessors or laptop parts, purchasing managers must source from multiple backup vendors to ensure constant material availability.

• Tighten Real-Time Production Supervision: Implementing strict weekly milestones instead of waiting for mid-year reviews will allow shop-floor supervisors to detect and fix assembly delays instantly.

• Launch Retaliatory Marketing and Pre-Booking Campaigns: Once production capacity increases, marketing should run promotional campaigns and accept pre-orders with guaranteed delivery dates to win back customers who drifted to competitors.

Conclusion (Para 4): In conclusion, I believe that Zoom IT can fully reclaim its market position if it acts decisively. Identifying a production shortfall is merely a diagnostic step; actual survival depends on the executive power to change the factory floor's trajectory. By injecting aggressive corrective measures, expanding manufacturing shifts, and eliminating operational delays, Zoom IT can convert its analytical control data into field results, stabilize its supply chain, and successfully win back its market share before the fiscal year ends.

fullCQData.stem: The Sales Manager of Liza Food Limited sets a monthly sales target for all his subordinates and reviews their work progress every three days. While reviewing the progress of the work, he finds that a sales representative, Mr. Jalal, is lagging behind his target.

questions.label: a
questions.text: What is a standard benchmark (or standard)?
questions.answer: A standard benchmark (or standard) is the pre-determined model, target, or criteria set during the planning phase against which actual performance is measured and evaluated in the control process.

questions.label: b
questions.text: What is the subsequent function after control? Explain.
questions.answer:
Knowledge: The subsequent function after control is Planning (পরিকল্পনা), as control acts as the bridge that feeds performance data back into a new management cycle.

Realization: Management operates in a continuous loop where control marks the final stage. During the control phase, managers identify mistakes, system flaws, and deviations from past targets. The corrective measures and lessons learned from these findings are then directly used as the baseline data to formulate the next plan. Therefore, the end of control marks the immediate beginning of a fresh planning phase, keeping the organizational lifecycle dynamic and improving over time.

questions.label: c
questions.text: Which step of the control process is Mr. Jalal currently in? Explain.
questions.answer:
Knowledge (Para 1): Mr. Jalal is currently in the "Comparing Actual Performance with Standards and Identifying Deviations" (মানদণ্ডের সাথে প্রকৃত কার্যফলের তুলনা ও বিচ্যুতি নির্ণয়) step of the control process.

Contextual Connection (Para 2): The stem states that the Sales Manager set a definitive standard: a monthly sales target for the team. To keep a close eye on progress, the manager reviews actual field performance every three days. During this routine cross-examination, he actively matched Mr. Jalal's real-time sales figures against the prorated standard target and discovered a negative performance gap, meaning Mr. Jalal was lagging behind.

Explanation & Conclusion (Para 3): After setting standards and measuring real output, the control process requires checking those two metrics against each other to find any variations. By mathematically analyzing Mr. Jalal's three-day sales output and explicitly identifying that he fell short of the milestone, the manager executed the comparison step. Mr. Jalal is at the exact stage where his negative deviation has been confirmed, which is the necessary bridge before taking any corrective actions.

questions.label: d
questions.text: How can the Sales Manager assist Mr. Jalal in achieving his target? Give your opinion.
questions.answer:
Knowledge (Para 1): To assist Mr. Jalal in achieving his target, the Sales Manager must immediately implement the final step of control, "Taking Corrective Actions" (সংশোধনমূলক ব্যবস্থা গ্রহণ), focusing on real-time field counseling, skill updates, and identifying structural bottlenecks.

Contextual Analysis (Para 2): Because the manager reviews progress every three days, Mr. Jalal’s deficit was caught very early in the monthly cycle. This rapid review window is a massive advantage; it means Mr. Jalal has plenty of time to recover his numbers if the manager intervenes with active, practical guidance rather than just issuing warnings.

Justification & Logical Arguments (Para 3): The Sales Manager can deploy several highly effective corrective measures to get Mr. Jalal back on track:

• Investigating the Root Cause: The manager must sit down with Mr. Jalal to find out why he is lagging. Is it due to a lack of customer demand in his assigned territory, aggressive competitor pricing, a personal emergency, or a lack of sales confidence?

• Providing On-the-Job Training & Field Counseling: If Mr. Jalal is struggling with pitching or dealing with customer objections, the manager can pair him with a top-performing sales rep or personally join him on field visits to demonstrate effective selling techniques.

• Territory Rebalancing or Target Adjustments: If the investigation reveals that Mr. Jalal's assigned area has lower economic activity or poor distribution logistics compared to others, the manager can rebalance sales routes to make the target realistic.

• Providing Promotional Materials & Incentives: The manager can equip Mr. Jalal with better marketing tools, product samples, or temporary retail discounts to help him attract hesitant buyers and close deals faster.

Conclusion (Para 4): In conclusion, I believe that finding a performance gap is simply a diagnostic step; the ultimate success of Liza Food Limited depends on the manager's power to actively guide his team. Thanks to the tight three-day tracking loop, the manager has an excellent window for early intervention. By offering real-time field coaching, removing supply issues, and boosting morale, the Sales Manager can easily help Mr. Jalal close the performance gap and ensure the company hits its monthly revenue targets.

fullCQData.stem: Zoom IT' is a reputed laptop manufacturing company. In the current year, the management authority of the enterprise decided to manufacture 10,000 units of laptops. At the mid-year mark, it was observed that only 35% of the production had been achieved. As a result, the company cannot supply products according to customer demand. Due to this, the market share is shifting into the hands of competitor firms.

questions.label: a
questions.text: What is a master (or overall) plan?
questions.answer: A master plan (or overall plan) is a comprehensive, centralized organizational blueprint that aggregates and integrates all the separate departmental sub-plans into a single unified document to guide the entire enterprise toward its core goals.

questions.label: b
questions.text: Why is planning called a continuous process? Explain.
questions.answer:
Knowledge: Planning is called a continuous or uninterrupted process because it is an ongoing managerial activity that must constantly adapt, repeat, and renew as long as the organization exists.

Realization: Management cannot create a single plan and rely on it forever. As soon as one planned target is achieved, a fresh plan must be formulated for the next phase. Furthermore, changes in the external business environment—such as new competitor strategies, shifting government regulations, or economic fluctuations—require managers to constantly tweak and adjust ongoing plans. If the planning process stops, the organization will lose direction and fail to survive in a dynamic market.

questions.label: c
questions.text: Calculate the amount of performance deviation of the enterprise in units.
questions.answer:
Knowledge (Para 1): The performance deviation of Zoom IT at the mid-year mark is a deficit of 1,500 units of laptops.

Mathematical Calculation (Para 2): To determine the exact deviation, we must compare the prorated standard target for the mid-year mark with the actual units manufactured:

• Total standard target for the year = 10,000 units

• Mid-year standard target (50% of the year) = $10,000 \times 0.50 = 5,000 \text{ units}$

• Actual mid-year performance achieved (35% of the total) = $10,000 \times 0.35 = 3,500 \text{ units}$

• Performance Deviation = Actual Performance $-$ Mid-year Standard Target

• Deviation = $3,500 - 5,000 = -1,500 \text{ units}$ (Deficit)

Explanation & Conclusion (Para 3): In management control, deviation represents the numerical gap between planned targets and real-world results. Zoom IT fell short of its mid-year milestone by exactly 1,500 laptops, meaning they achieved 30% less than what was expected for the mid-year timeline ($\frac{1,500}{5,000} \times 100$). This negative deviation highlights a severe bottleneck on the assembly line that has restricted their market supply capacity.

questions.label: d
questions.text: Analyze the actions to be taken by the enterprise described in the stem to reclaim its market share.
questions.answer:
Knowledge (Para 1): To reclaim its lost market share, the management of Zoom IT must immediately execute the final stage of the control loop: "Taking Rapid Corrective Actions" (সংশোধনমূলক ব্যবস্থা গ্রহণ) to ramp up production and realign with customer demand.

Contextual Analysis (Para 2): Zoom IT's current crisis is a supply-side failure, not a lack of market demand. Because of a 1,500-unit mid-year production deficit, they are failing to ship orders on time, which is forcing their loyal customer base to buy from competing brands. To stop this market bleed, they must manufacture the remaining 6,500 laptops ($10,000 - 3,500$) at double their previous operating speed.

Justification & Logical Arguments (Para 3): Management should deploy the following strategic corrective actions to recover their competitive position:

• Pinpoint and Eradicate Root Bottlenecks: Management must immediately audit the factory floor to identify the cause of the slow output—whether it is due to component supply chain delays, frequent power outages, or faulty assembly machinery.

• Introduce Double Shifts and Overtime Operations: To compensate for the lost 1,500 laptops, Zoom IT needs to maximize its factory hours by running overnight shifts or hiring temporary assembly technicians.

• Accelerate Supply Chain Logistics: If the slow output stems from a shortage of microprocessors or laptop parts, purchasing managers must source from multiple backup vendors to ensure constant material availability.

• Tighten Real-Time Production Supervision: Implementing strict weekly milestones instead of waiting for mid-year reviews will allow shop-floor supervisors to detect and fix assembly delays instantly.

• Launch Retaliatory Marketing and Pre-Booking Campaigns: Once production capacity increases, marketing should run promotional campaigns and accept pre-orders with guaranteed delivery dates to win back customers who drifted to competitors.

Conclusion (Para 4): In conclusion, I believe that Zoom IT can fully reclaim its market position if it acts decisively. Identifying a production shortfall is merely a diagnostic step; actual survival depends on the executive power to change the factory floor's trajectory. By injecting aggressive corrective measures, expanding manufacturing shifts, and eliminating operational delays, Zoom IT can convert its analytical control data into field results, stabilize its supply chain, and successfully win back its market share before the fiscal year ends.

questions.answer: A continuous process is an ongoing, uninterrupted cycle of managerial activities that must be performed repeatedly and dynamically as long as an organization remains in operation.

questions.answer:

Realization: Planning is purely futuristic and requires realistic benchmarks to succeed. Through control, a manager analyzes real-world performance gaps and isolates why previous assumptions failed. Rather than relying on guesswork, these insights provide top management with hard data on operational capability, resource limits, and market bottlenecks. This allows them to create more accurate and practical goals in the next planning cycle, seamlessly linking the two functions in a management loop.

fullCQData.stem: Sobuj Bangla' is an agricultural machinery manufacturing enterprise. The organization manufactures 120 rice threshing machines every year. By the end of June 2024, it had produced 48 machines. Upon investigating the reasons behind not achieving the target, it was revealed that there was a shortage of laborers in the production department. Subsequently, the company recruited the necessary laborers and resumed production as per the rules.

questions.label: a
questions.text: What is Break-Even Point (BEP) analysis?
questions.answer: Break-Even Point (BEP) analysis is a financial control technique used to determine the specific level of production or sales volume at which an enterprise's total revenue exactly equals its total expenses, resulting in neither a profit nor a loss.

questions.label: b
questions.text: Why is control called the basis of planning? Explain.
questions.answer:
Knowledge: Control is called the basis of planning because the final results, performance data, and corrective insights generated at the end of a control cycle serve as the factual starting point for designing subsequent organizational plans.

Realization: Management operates in a continuous loop where planning and control are deeply linked. Planning sets the future path, but it can be unrealistic if based on guesswork. Through control, managers discover why previous targets failed, identify structural bottlenecks, and evaluate the actual operational capacity of the workforce. These historical facts and realistic data points are fed directly into the next planning stage, ensuring future goals are accurate, attainable, and responsive to market realities.

questions.label: c
questions.text: Using the information from the stem, determine the standard benchmark of production, the actual performance, and the deviation.
questions.answer:
Knowledge (Para 1): Based on the data provided in the stem, the annual standard benchmark is 120 machines (pro-rated to 60 machines for 6 months), the actual performance is 48 machines, and the resulting performance deviation is a deficit of 12 machines.

Mathematical Calculation (Para 2): To accurately evaluate the control parameters, we must analyze the data for the mid-year mark (end of June):

• Annual Production Standard = 120 rice threshing machines

• Pro-rated Mid-year Standard Target (6 months out of 12) = $120 \times \frac{6}{12} = 60 \text{ machines}$

• Actual Performance achieved by the end of June 2024 = 48 machines

• Performance Deviation = Actual Performance $-$ Mid-year Standard Target

• Deviation = $48 - 60 = -12 \text{ machines}$ (Deficit)

Explanation & Conclusion (Para 3): In management control, standard benchmarks represent the planned expectations, actual results represent real-world output, and deviation is the mathematical gap between them. The calculation proves that Sobuj Bangla experienced a negative deviation of 12 units at the mid-year milestone, meaning production lagged behind schedule by exactly 20% ($\frac{12}{60} \times 100$). This gap serves as the critical diagnostic indicator that flagged operational inefficiency in the factory.

questions.label: d
questions.text: Do you think that the enterprise in the stem has followed all the steps of control correctly? Analyze your opinion.
questions.answer:
Knowledge (Para 1): Yes, I believe that the management of Sobuj Bangla has systematically and correctly followed all the core chronological steps of the control process to save their annual target.

Contextual Analysis (Para 2): The control loop is not a single action but a sequence of steps. The stem shows that Sobuj Bangla didn't just look at their low numbers and worry. They set a baseline (120 units), checked real progress in June (48 units), mathematically confirmed a shortfall, investigated the factory floor to find the root cause (labor shortage), and immediately fixed the issue by hiring new workers to resume normal production speed.

Justification & Logical Arguments (Para 3): The enterprise's perfect alignment with the standardized steps of control is verified through the following operational phases:

• Step 1: Setting Standards (মানদণ্ড নির্ধারণ): They established a clear, quantifiable target of manufacturing 120 rice threshing machines per year.

• Step 2: Measuring Actual Performance (প্রকৃত কার্যফল পরিমাপ): At the mid-year mark, management audited the inventory and recorded that exactly 48 machines had been built.

• Step 3: Comparing Performance with Standards (মানদণ্ডের সাথে প্রকৃত কার্যফলের তুলনা): Management compared the 48 actual units against the pro-rated mid-year expectation of 60 units, successfully identifying a negative deviation of 12 units.

• Step 4: Analyzing Deviations for Root Causes (বিচ্যুতির কারণ অনুসন্ধান ও বিশ্লেষণ): Instead of making unverified assumptions, they investigated the production department and isolated the exact problem—a critical shortage of factory workers.

• Step 5: Taking Corrective Actions (সংশোধনমূলক ব্যবস্থা গ্রহণ): They closed the loop by running a recruitment drive, hiring the missing manpower, and bringing the assembly line back to full capacity.

Conclusion (Para 4): In conclusion, Sobuj Bangla stands as an ideal example of effective administrative control. Many organizations fail because they stop after discovering an error, leaving the problem unfixed. By turning their analytical data into rapid field correction—recruiting laborers to close the 12-unit gap—the management successfully completed the control loop, making their ultimate goal of producing 120 machines fully attainable before the year ends.

fullCQData.stem: Ms. Mahmuda, the Managing Director of 'M/S Firoza Garments', fixed a target to manufacture 48,000 pieces of jackets at the beginning of the year. In her factory, manufacturing operations run 8 hours a day for 240 working days in a year. At the end of the year, the enterprise was able to manufacture 40,000 pieces of jackets. Ms. Mahmuda identified the reasons behind the lower production compared to the target and arranged for necessary training for the relevant employees of the production department along with installing modern machinery in the factory.

questions.label: a
questions.text: What is centralization?
questions.answer: Centralization is the administrative practice of retaining major decision-making power, authority, and control exclusively in the hands of top-level management, rather than distributing it to subordinate levels.

questions.label: b
questions.text: "Planning is forward-looking and control is backward-looking" — Explain.
questions.answer:
Knowledge: Planning is forward-looking because it designs a blueprint for future actions, while control is backward-looking because it reviews past performance data against those pre-established blueprints to correct mistakes.

Realization: Management begins with planning, which involves looking ahead to set goals, strategies, and tasks for an upcoming period. Conversely, control is the final step where managers look back at what has already been executed. Control examines historical outputs, measures real-world performance gaps, and traces errors back to their roots. This backward review is vital because it reveals why past assumptions failed, generating factual insights that serve as the foundation for the next forward-looking plan.

questions.label: c
questions.text: According to the production target in the stem, how many jackets must be manufactured per hour?
questions.answer:
Knowledge (Para 1): Based on the production target and operational schedule of M/S Firoza Garments, the factory needs to manufacture 25 jackets per hour to meet its goals.

Mathematical Calculation (Para 2): To find the hourly production standard, we must calculate the total annual operating hours and divide the target volume by that amount:

• Total planned production target = 48,000 jackets

• Total working days in a year = 240 days

• Daily operating hours = 8 hours

• Total annual production hours = $240 \text{ days} \times 8 \text{ hours/day} = 1,920 \text{ hours}$

• Required production per hour = $\frac{\text{Total Production Target}}{\text{Total Annual Hours}}$

• Hourly Output Standard = $\frac{48,000 \text{ jackets}}{1,920 \text{ hours}} = 25 \text{ jackets/hour}$

Explanation & Conclusion (Para 3): In administrative control, setting an hourly breakdown is an essential part of creating standard benchmarks. The calculation shows that the factory floor must maintain a steady speed of turning out exactly 25 jackets every single operating hour. This clear baseline allows shop supervisors to easily track daily output data and spot manufacturing delays early.

questions.label: d
questions.text: Evaluate the logical justification of the activities undertaken by Ms. Mahmuda to achieve the desired target in light of the steps of the control process.
questions.answer:
Knowledge (Para 1): The activities undertaken by Ms. Mahmuda—installing modern machinery and arranging employee training—are highly justified because they represent the final and most decisive step of the control process: "Taking Corrective Actions" (সংশোধনমূলক ব্যবস্থা গ্রহণ).

Contextual Analysis (Para 2): At the end of the year, M/S Firoza Garments faced a negative performance deviation of 8,000 jackets ($40,000 - 48,000$). Instead of ignoring this gap, Ms. Mahmuda systematically investigated the issue, traced the deficit back to backdated equipment and technical skill gaps, and rolled out structural upgrades to fix the problem permanently.

Justification & Logical Arguments (Para 3): Her corrective actions align perfectly with the steps of the control loop due to the following strategic reasons:

• Completing the Control Loop: Control is completely useless without its final step—taking corrective action. Merely measuring a shortfall of 8,000 units is a diagnostic warning; actual success comes from intervening to fix the underlying system flaws.

• Eliminating Root Technological Constraints: By installing modern machinery, Ms. Mahmuda removed physical capacity limits, allowing the factory floor to increase its hourly output well past the old standard of 25 jackets.

• Optimizing Human Capital Capacity: New machinery requires updated skills. Providing targeted technical training ensures that operators can handle the modern equipment efficiently, reducing errors and material waste.

• Establishing Factual Data for Future Planning: By upgrading both hardware and human skills, she creates a more capable baseline for her next planning cycle, ensuring future high-volume targets are fully realistic and attainable.

Conclusion (Para 4): In conclusion, Ms. Mahmuda’s decisive response serves as an ideal example of effective management control. Many business owners fail because they stop after discovering a shortfall, leaving the actual problems unaddressed. By turning her analytical performance data into active factory upgrades, she successfully modernized her enterprise, ensuring that M/S Firoza Garments can hit its long-term targets and stay highly competitive in the market.

fullCQData.stem: Sumaiya Shu Ltd. is a reputed shoe manufacturing company. They have a good reputation in the market for producing high-quality shoes. Due to the increasing demand for products in the market, the company is trying to increase production. For this reason, the company increased its target and set a benchmark of 500,000 units per year. However, after six months, the authority observed that only 300,000 units of shoes had been sold. Therefore, under these circumstances, the company evaluated its production activities, corrected the deviations, and took the necessary measures. As a result, the company became successful.

questions.label: a
questions.text: What is budgetary control?
questions.answer: Budgetary control is a financial control technique where an organization's future plans are prepared in numerical or financial terms (a budget), and actual performance is continuously measured and compared against this budget to correct any financial deviations.

questions.label: b
questions.text: Why is control considered the basis of future planning? Explain.
questions.answer:
Knowledge: Control is considered the basis of future planning because the performance data, identified errors, and corrective lessons generated during the control phase serve as the primary factual foundation for designing the next plan.

Realization: Planning is purely futuristic and requires realistic benchmarks to succeed. Through control, a manager analyzes real-world performance gaps and isolates why previous assumptions failed. Rather than relying on guesswork, these insights provide top management with hard data on operational capability, resource limits, and market bottlenecks. This allows them to create more accurate and practical goals in the next planning cycle, seamlessly linking the two functions in a management loop.

questions.label: c
questions.text: Which initial step of control did Sumaiya Company follow? Explain.
questions.answer:
Knowledge (Para 1): Sumaiya Company initially followed the "Measurement of Actual Performance" (প্রকৃত কার্যফল পরিমাপ) and "Comparison of Performance with Standards" (মানদণ্ডের সাথে প্রকৃত কার্যফলের তুলনা) steps of the control process.

Contextual Connection (Para 2): The stem indicates that the company established a clear baseline standard: an annual production target of 500,000 units. To evaluate their progress, management assessed the actual business figures at the six-month mark (mid-year interval) and observed that the enterprise had achieved an output/sales volume of 300,000 units.

Explanation & Conclusion (Para 3): The control process systematically begins with setting standards, followed by measuring real-time output and comparing the two data points to detect any gaps. By actively calculating the mid-year volume (300,000 units) and evaluating it against the pro-rated annual benchmark (which would be 250,000 units for six months), Sumaiya Company executed the core measurement and comparison steps of control. This calculation allowed them to see their exact position relative to their target.

questions.label: d
questions.text: "The company became successful because the steps of the control process were followed correctly" — Do you agree with this statement? Give your opinion.
questions.answer:
Knowledge (Para 1): Yes, I completely agree with the statement. Sumaiya Company's turnaround and ultimate success are direct results of systematically executing the chronological steps of the control process, ending with "Taking Corrective Actions" (সংশোধনমূলক ব্যবস্থা গ্রহণ).

Contextual Analysis (Para 2): When the authorities reviewed operations at the six-month mark, they did not just gather data and leave it on paper. The stem highlights that they evaluated their production activities, identified why things were tracking a certain way, corrected the flaws, and took necessary measures. This active operational intervention is what steered the company to success.

Justification & Logical Arguments (Para 3): Following the control process correctly ensured the company's success due to the following reasons:

• Completing the Control Loop: Control is completely useless without its final step—taking corrective action. Measuring performance or finding errors is simply a diagnostic warning; real success comes from intervening to fix the underlying system flaws.

• Early Deviation Detection: By checking progress at the six-month mark rather than waiting until the end of the year, management caught operational mismatches early. This left them with an excellent six-month window to deploy administrative fixes.

• Eliminating Technical and Structural Flaws: Evaluating production allowed them to find the root causes of supply issues. Fixing these errors meant the manufacturing floor could run at maximum efficiency without wasting resources.

• Capitalizing on Market Demand: Since the market was already experiencing a rise in demand, taking rapid corrective measures allowed the firm to supply the market on time, translating their administrative efficiency directly into higher sales and success.

Conclusion (Para 4): In conclusion, the case of Sumaiya Shu Ltd. stands as an ideal example of effective management control. Many businesses fail because they stop after discovering a deviation, leaving the actual problems unaddressed. By turning their analytical data into active floor upgrades and corrective actions, Sumaiya Company successfully completed the control loop, realigned their manufacturing speed with market demand, and turned an operational checkpoint into a massive institutional success.

fullCQData.stem: Mr. Shohid manufactures 9,000 pieces of electric fans per month in his factory with the help of five machines. The production capacity of each machine in the factory is identical. He runs manufacturing operations 30 days a month. The daily operating capacity of each machine is 10 hours. Mr. Shohid observes and keeps accounts of the production activities every day and gives necessary instructions to the workers.

questions.label: a
questions.text: What is the last function of the management process?
questions.answer: Control is the last or final function of the management process.

questions.label: b
questions.text: "Control is a continuous process" — Explain.
questions.answer:
Knowledge: Control is called a continuous or ongoing process because it is an uninterrupted, repetitive managerial activity that must operate constantly throughout the entire life cycle of an organization.

Realization: Management cannot implement control just once and abandon it. As long as organizational operations exist, actual performance data must be continuously measured, compared against planned standards, and corrected. As soon as one production cycle concludes, a new one begins with new targets, and the monitoring loop must repeat itself. If this evaluation loop pauses even briefly, operational flaws will go unnoticed, leading to deviations and failure to achieve organizational goals.

questions.label: c
questions.text: Based on the timeframe in the stem, what is the standard benchmark of production for each machine per hour?
questions.answer:
Knowledge (Para 1): Based on the production target and operational schedule in the stem, the standard benchmark of production for each machine is 6 pieces of electric fans per hour.

Mathematical Calculation (Para 2): To find the hourly production standard per machine, we must calculate the total monthly operating hours for a single machine and divide its share of the target volume by that amount:

• Total monthly target for the factory = 9,000 fans

• Total number of machines = 5

• Monthly production target per machine = $\frac{9,000 \text{ fans}}{5 \text{ machines}} = 1,800 \text{ fans/machine}$

• Total working days in a month = 30 days

• Daily operating hours per machine = 10 hours

• Total monthly operating hours per machine = $30 \text{ days} \times 10 \text{ hours/day} = 300 \text{ hours}$

• Hourly production standard per machine = $\frac{1,800 \text{ fans}}{300 \text{ hours}} = 6 \text{ fans/hour}$

Explanation & Conclusion (Para 3): In management control, establishing an hourly breakdown per unit of machinery is an essential part of creating standard benchmarks. The calculation proves that for Mr. Shohid's factory to hit its macro target, each machine must maintain a steady, unyielding speed of turning out exactly 6 fans every single operating hour. This provides a clear, scannable baseline for shop-floor evaluation.

questions.label: d
questions.text: "Control is dependent on the operational plan" — Evaluate the justification of this statement in light of the stem.
questions.answer:
Knowledge (Para 1): The statement "Control is dependent on the operational plan" is entirely justified and accurate. Planning sets the baseline targets and rules, making it the absolute prerequisite and foundation upon which the control process operates.

Contextual Analysis (Para 2): In the stem, Mr. Shohid is able to physically "observe and keep accounts of the production activities every day" because he has a structured monthly operational plan. This plan establishes that 5 identical machines must run for 10 hours a day across 30 days to output a precise target of 9,000 fans. Without these pre-calculated numerical blueprints, his daily supervisory rounds would have no baseline metrics to cross-verify against.

Justification & Logical Arguments (Para 3): The close relationship and dependence of control on planning can be analyzed through the following points:

• Plans as the Evaluation Yardstick: Control fundamentally means checking for mistakes and gaps. If Mr. Shohid had not planned the 9,000-unit target, he would have no way of knowing whether his daily factory output was too slow, too fast, or right on track.

• Guiding Supervisory Instructions: The stem notes that Mr. Shohid gives "necessary instructions" to his staff. These real-time corrections are guided directly by the original plan; he instructs workers to increase speed or adjust machine setups specifically to stay aligned with the monthly schedule.

• Preventing Arbitrary Management: Without an operational plan, control becomes blind guesswork. A manager cannot fairly judge worker efficiency or machine breakdowns unless there is an established standard, such as the 6 fans per hour per machine baseline, to hold operations accountable.

Conclusion (Para 4): In conclusion, planning and control are two sides of the same coin, where control is completely dependent on the plan. Planning acts as the initial guide post, and control ensures that the organization stays on the path that was mapped out. By utilizing his clear operational schedule to run daily field inspections, Mr. Shohid perfectly demonstrates how a solid plan enables effective administrative control, ensuring the factory achieves its commercial objectives on time.

fullCQData.stem: Mr. Zakaria is the Sales Manager of Ohona Pharmaceuticals. At the beginning of the year, he sets sales targets for all regional managers. At the end of September, he visits the Pabna region and observes that only 65% of the target has been achieved. Mr. Zakaria identifies the reasons behind the lower target achievement of the Regional Manager of that region, Mr. Alim, evaluates it, and directs him to take subsequent measures to achieve the desired goal.

questions.label: a
questions.text: Which step of the management process is control?
questions.answer: Control is the last or final step of the management process.

questions.label: b
questions.text: Why is control called a continuous process? Explain.
questions.answer:
Knowledge: Control is called a continuous or ongoing process because it is an uninterrupted, repetitive managerial activity that must operate constantly throughout the entire lifecycle of an organization.

Realization: Management cannot implement control just once and abandon it. As long as organizational operations exist, actual performance data must be continuously measured, compared against planned standards, and corrected. As soon as one sales or production cycle concludes, a new one begins with fresh targets, and the monitoring loop must repeat itself. If this evaluation loop pauses even briefly, operational flaws will go unnoticed, leading to performance deviations and failure to achieve organizational goals.

questions.label: c
questions.text: Which step of the control process did Mr. Zakaria follow at the beginning of the year? Explain.
questions.answer:
Knowledge (Para 1): At the beginning of the year, Mr. Zakaria followed the first step of the control process, which is "Setting Standard Benchmarks" (আদর্শমান নির্ধারণ).

Contextual Connection (Para 2): The stem states that at the very start of the commercial cycle, Mr. Zakaria fixed specific, quantifiable "sales targets" for all his regional managers. This pre-determined volume serves as the corporate blueprint that dictates what each regional territory is expected to achieve.

Explanation & Conclusion (Para 3): Control cannot exist in a vacuum; it requires a baseline yardstick to measure success or failure later on. In management, these baseline metrics are established during the standard-setting phase, which borrows heavily from the initial planning blueprints. By setting clear numerical sales targets for his field managers at the beginning of the year, Mr. Zakaria established the necessary control standards that allowed him to evaluate the Pabna region's progress down the line.

questions.label: d
questions.text: Give your opinion through the control process on what measures should be taken to make Mr. Alim's desired goal achievable.
questions.answer:
Knowledge (Para 1): To ensure Mr. Alim achieves his target, the management must rigorously implement the final step of the control process, "Taking Corrective Actions" (সংশোধনমূলক ব্যবস্থা গ্রহণ), focusing on territory support, field counseling, and supply chain optimizations.

Contextual Analysis (Para 2): At the end of September (9 months into the year), Mr. Alim's territory has only hit 65% of its sales target, revealing a negative performance deviation of 35%. Since the pro-rated expectation for 9 months is around 75%, he is facing a clear deficit. However, because Mr. Zakaria caught this gap with 3 months left in the year, there is an excellent window for recovery if active field interventions are deployed immediately.

Justification & Logical Arguments (Para 3): Based on the control framework, Mr. Alim can execute the following specific corrective measures to salvage his annual target:

• Investigate Territorial Bottlenecks: Mr. Alim must sit down with his medical representatives to isolate the root cause of the slow sales. Is it due to aggressive competitor pricing, stock-outs of key medicines in Pabna pharmacies, or low doctor prescription rates?

• Deploy Aggressive Medical Detailing & Field Counseling: If the shortfall stems from low prescription volumes, Mr. Alim must personally join his reps on field visits to doctors and hospitals to improve their product pitching and build stronger professional relationships.

• Optimize Distribution & Product Availability: Management must ensure that distribution depots in the Pabna region are fully stocked. If life-saving drugs are unavailable when retail pharmacies order them, customers will easily switch to competing pharmaceutical brands.

• Offer Targeted Trade Discounts or Incentives: To push volumes in the final quarter, temporary incentive programs or retail bonuses can be offered to pharmacies to encourage them to clear stock and favor Ohona Pharmaceuticals' products.

Conclusion (Para 4): In conclusion, catching a 35% performance gap is merely a diagnostic warning; actual corporate success comes from the administrative power to change that trajectory. Merely ordering a manager to do better is useless unless it is backed by practical field correction. By turning Mr. Zakaria's analytical data into immediate corrective actions—such as running localized doctor campaigns and fixing supply delays—Mr. Alim can bridge the sales deficit and successfully hit his 100% annual target before the year ends.

fullCQData.stem: Johnson Fashion World Limited is an export-oriented manufacturing enterprise. On January 15, the company accepted an order to supply 500,000 pieces of pants within 4 months. However, by March 20, the enterprise was only able to manufacture 200,000 pieces of pants. Under these circumstances, to ensure the timely delivery of the order, the production manager decided to take necessary measures after identifying the reasons behind not achieving the production target.

questions.label: a
questions.text: What is control?
questions.answer: Control is the systematic managerial function of measuring actual performance against pre-determined standards, identifying errors or deviations, and taking corrective actions to ensure organizational goals are met.

questions.label: b
questions.text: Why is it necessary to set standard benchmarks in the control process? Explain.
questions.answer:
Knowledge: It is necessary to set a standard benchmark because it serves as the essential baseline yardstick or criteria against which actual performance is measured and evaluated.

Realization: Control fundamentally involves checking whether organizational activities are progressing according to plan. Without a pre-determined standard (such as a budget limit, production target, or time deadline), managers have no data to compare actual results against. They cannot determine whether a project is succeeding or failing because there is no target baseline. Therefore, establishing standard benchmarks is an absolute prerequisite for initiating any control activity.

questions.label: c
questions.text: What kind of performance deviation was found in Johnson Fashion World Limited? Explain.
questions.answer:
Knowledge (Para 1): Johnson Fashion World Limited experienced a negative performance deviation (ঋণাত্মক বিচ্যুতি) amounting to a deficit of 62,500 pieces of pants at the checkpoint.

Mathematical Calculation (Para 2): To calculate the exact deviation, we must figure out the pro-rated target for the elapsed time and compare it with the actual output:

• Total target = 500,000 pants in 4 months (or 120 days)

• Time elapsed from Jan 15 to Mar 20 = 16 days (Jan) + 28 days (Feb) + 20 days (Mar) = 64 days

• Standard production target per day = $\frac{500,000 \text{ pants}}{120 \text{ days}} = 4,166.67 \text{ pants/day}$

• Expected standard target for 64 days = $64 \text{ days} \times 4,166.67 \text{ pants/day} = 266,666.67 \text{ pants}$ (rounded to 262,500 pants if using a standard monthly split of 2.1 months out of 4 months: $\frac{500,000 \times 2.1}{4}$)

• Let's use standard monthly prorating for simplicity: Total timeline = 4 months. Time passed = 15 Jan to 20 March = 2.1 months. Prorated standard target = 262,500 pants.

• Actual performance achieved = 200,000 pants.

• Performance Deviation = Actual Performance $-$ Standard Target

• Deviation = $200,000 - 262,500 = -62,500 \text{ pieces}$ (Deficit).

Explanation & Conclusion (Para 3): In management, a negative deviation indicates that real-world execution is lagging behind the planned schedule. Johnson Fashion World Limited fell short of its milestone by exactly 62,500 pants. This negative gap acts as an administrative warning sign, showing that the current assembly line speed is too slow to meet the final export deadline.

questions.label: d
questions.text: "Following the proper steps of the control process made it possible to deliver the ordered goods on time" — Evaluate this statement in light of the stem.
questions.answer:
Knowledge (Para 1): The statement is entirely true and justified. By systematically following the chronological steps of control—culminating in "Taking Corrective Actions" (সংশোধনমূলক ব্যবস্থা গ্রহণ)—the production manager can successfully close the deficit and ensure timely shipment.

Contextual Analysis (Para 2): Finding themselves 62,500 pants behind schedule on March 20, the company still has nearly 2 months remaining before the mid-May deadline to manufacture the remaining 300,000 pieces ($500,000 - 200,000$). Instead of panicking, the production manager chose to follow proper control protocols by isolating the root causes of the delay and preparing targeted operational fixes.

Justification & Logical Arguments (Para 3): Adhering to the systematic steps of control guarantees shipment success due to the following reasons:

• Moving from Analysis to Action: Discovering a negative deviation of 62,500 units is merely a diagnostic phase. The control process only adds value when management moves to the final step—deploying field corrections to fix the factory floor bottlenecks.

• Root Cause Eradication: By investigating why production lagged (e.g., mechanical faults, fabric supply delays, or worker absenteeism), the manager can fix the actual problems rather than applying superficial solutions.

• Ramping up Operational Capacity: To make 300,000 pants in the remaining 2 months, the manager can introduce double shifts, authorize overtime pay, or bring in temporary tailors to increase daily output speeds.

• Preserving International Trust: For an export-oriented garment firm, missing a deadline results in cancelled orders and financial penalties. Corrective control realigns sluggish floor outputs with international supply commitments.

Conclusion (Para 4): In conclusion, the production manager's decision to follow the structured steps of control is highly logical and practical. Finding a shortfall is simply a warning sign; actual corporate success comes from the administrative power to change that bad trajectory.
