By turning analytical control data into rapid interventions—such as increasing floor supervision and running extra shifts—the management can successfully speed up production, eliminate the 62,500-unit deficit, and deliver the shipment on time.

fullCQData.stem: Mr. Masum has established a dairy farm. After completing all necessary arrangements, he started operations with a target production level of 900 liters of milk per month. However, at the end of the month, he observed that only 700 liters of milk had been produced. Later, through close and intensive supervision, he discovered that the workers were cunningly misappropriating the produced milk. Consequently, he terminated two workers and hired new staff.

questions.label: a
questions.text: What is the basis of control?
questions.answer: Planning (পরিকল্পনা) is the basis of control.

questions.label: b
questions.text: What is meant by standard benchmark (or standard)? Explain.
questions.answer:
Knowledge: A standard benchmark (or standard) refers to the pre-determined model, target, or yardstick established during the planning phase, against which the actual performance of an organization is measured and evaluated.

Realization: Standards serve as the corporate baseline for judging operational success or failure. These metrics can be qualitative (such as product quality or customer satisfaction) or quantitative (such as a target of producing 1,000 units, or a budget limit). Without a clear, predetermined standard, it is impossible to evaluate real-world performance because there is no target baseline to check against. Therefore, setting standard benchmarks acts as the fundamental starting point of the control function.

questions.label: c
questions.text: Which step of the control process did Mr. Masum adopt to confirm the shortfall in production? Explain.
questions.answer:
Knowledge (Para 1): Mr. Masum adopted the second step of the control process, which is "Measuring Actual Performance" (প্রকৃত কার্যফল পরিমাপ), and compared it with his target to confirm the production shortfall.

Contextual Connection (Para 2): The stem indicates that Mr. Masum set an initial monthly production target of 900 liters of milk. At the end of the month, he evaluated his actual operational results and recorded that the farm had generated exactly 700 liters of milk. By putting these two real-world figures side by side, he pinpointed a clear deficit.

Explanation & Conclusion (Para 3): Performance measurement is the diagnostic phase of control where managers collect real-time field data and contrast it against the original planning targets. It is through this step that deviations become visible. By measuring the real yield (700 liters) against his benchmark (900 liters), Mr. Masum was able to mathematically prove a negative performance deviation of 200 liters, thereby confirming that the dairy farm was lagging behind its planned capacity.

questions.label: d
questions.text: Analyze the managerial function that Mr. Masum has executed appropriately in light of the stem.
questions.answer:
Knowledge (Para 1): Mr. Masum has flawlessly executed "Control" (নিয়ন্ত্রণ)—the final and corrective function of management—by identifying operational theft, isolating the root cause, and deploying strict administrative discipline.

Contextual Analysis (Para 2): After recording a 200-liter shortfall, Mr. Masum did not simply ignore the data or guess the reason. He conducted intensive, close floor-supervision to uncover the root cause, discovering that internal workers were stealthily stealing the milk. To address this severe ethical breakdown, he immediately completed the control loop by firing two corrupt workers and bringing in fresh personnel.

Justification & Logical Arguments (Para 3): Mr. Masum’s actions perfectly illustrate why the control function is vital for an enterprise's survival:

• Moving from Analysis to Correction: Discovering a deficit is merely a diagnostic phase. The control process only adds value when management moves to the final step—deploying active interventions to fix the leakages.

• Protecting Corporate Assets: If Mr. Masum had only monitored the numbers without taking disciplinary actions, the workplace theft would have continued, leading to compounding financial losses and the eventual collapse of the dairy farm.

• Establishing Accountability: Firing the dishonest workers sends a clear message to the remaining staff. It restores workplace discipline, minimizes future inventory shrinkage, and ensures the farm can realistically hit its 900-liter baseline.

Conclusion (Para 4): In conclusion, Mr. Masum's execution of management principles is highly logical and complete. Finding a shortfall is simply a warning sign; actual corporate success comes from the administrative power to change that trajectory. By turning raw monitoring data into direct corrective action—firing the bad actors and restructuring his workforce—he successfully protected his business investment and paved the way to achieve his desired goals.

fullCQData.stem: Mrs. Fatiha is the production manager of an organization. She set a weekly production target of 100,000 units of products for her unit. At the end of the week, it was observed that the actual production of that unit was only 80,000 units. Under these circumstances, she is quite worried and is now considering revising the plan.

questions.label: a
questions.text: What is control?
questions.answer: Control is the systematic managerial function of measuring actual performance against pre-determined standards, identifying errors or deviations, and taking corrective actions to ensure organizational goals are met.

questions.label: b
questions.text: Why is control called the basis of the next plan? Explain.
questions.answer:
Knowledge: Control is called the basis of the next plan because the data, performance feedback, and corrective lessons gathered during the control stage provide the realistic foundation upon which future plans are designed.

Realization: Planning is the initial step of management, while control is the final step. However, management is a continuous cycle. When control reveals performance deviations, it investigates the root causes—such as unrealistic targets, resource shortages, or worker inefficiency. This vital diagnostic information ensures that the next planning cycle is realistic and grounded in actual field capability, rather than guesswork. Thus, control acts as a bridge, transforming past operational feedback into the starting point for future organizational strategies.

questions.label: c
questions.text: At which step of the control process did Mrs. Fatiha notice the production shortfall? Explain.
questions.answer:
Knowledge (Para 1): Mrs. Fatiha noticed the production shortfall during the third step of the control process, which is "Comparing Actual Performance with Standards" (আদর্শ মানের সাথে প্রকৃত কার্যফল তুলনাকরণ).

Contextual Connection (Para 2): According to the stem, Mrs. Fatiha had set a weekly standard target of 100,000 units. At the end of the cycle, she measured the real-world output and found it to be 80,000 units. By placing the actual performance side-by-side with her original plan, she mathematically exposed a negative deviation of 20,000 units ($80,000 - 100,000$).

Explanation & Conclusion (Para 3): Comparing performance is the specific diagnostic phase where managers contrast raw field data against pre-determined benchmarks to isolate gaps. Mrs. Fatiha could not have known there was a shortfall simply by looking at the 80,000 units alone; it was the active step of comparison against her 100,000-unit baseline that highlighted the 20,000-unit deficit and triggered her concern.

questions.label: d
questions.text: Provide the necessary advice to Mrs. Fatiha regarding the revision of the plan.
questions.answer:
Knowledge (Para 1): To successfully revise her plan, Mrs. Fatiha must transition from the diagnostic phase to "Taking Corrective Action" (সংশোধনমূলক ব্যবস্থা গ্রহণ) by thoroughly auditing her production bottlenecks before adjusting her targets or resources.

Contextual Analysis (Para 2): Facing a substantial 20% shortfall (20,000 units), Mrs. Fatiha is rightfully worried. However, simply lowering the target or panicking will not solve the underlying factory floor issues. She must use systematic control principles to investigate why the gap occurred and implement logical administrative fixes.

Justification & Strategic Advice (Para 3): To effectively restructure her planning framework, Mrs. Fatiha should adopt the following measures:

• Investigate the Root Causes: Before changing any numbers, she must determine if the deficit was caused by internal inefficiencies (such as machinery breakdowns, worker absenteeism, or raw material delays) or an inherently unrealistic initial target.

• Evaluate and Optimize Resources: If the target of 100,000 units is necessary to meet market demand, she should advise the company to scale up operational capacity—such as introducing overtime pay, repairing old machinery, or adding an extra production shift.

• Formulate a Realistic, Flexible Plan: If her audit reveals that the factory floor's maximum physical capacity is lower than expected, she must revise her next weekly plan to a realistic baseline (e.g., 85,000 units) to avoid overpromising to clients.

• Establish Milestone-Based Monitoring: Instead of checking results only at the end of the week, she should implement daily production check-ins to catch and fix minor delays before they compound into a major weekend shortfall.

Conclusion (Para 4): In conclusion, Mrs. Fatiha's inclination to revise her plan is highly logical, but it must be driven by data rather than anxiety. Identifying a shortfall is merely an administrative warning sign; actual corporate success comes from the power to change that trajectory. By turning her analytical control data into targeted interventions—either by boosting floor resources or adjusting future benchmarks to match real factory capacity—she will ensure the organization maintains steady, predictable, and highly efficient manufacturing outputs.

fullCQData.stem: Mr. Shimu is the owner of a garments factory. He fixed a target to manufacture 15,000 pieces of clothing per month for his enterprise. Despite utilizing the full production capacity, the production in January and February was 13,000 and 13,500 pieces respectively. Upon investigation, a shortage of skilled laborers and raw materials was found to be the underlying reason. Following the supply of necessary skilled workers and raw materials, the enterprise succeeded in achieving its desired target starting from March.

questions.label: a
questions.text: What is a terminal (or periodic) task?
questions.answer: A terminal (or periodic) task is an administrative activity or evaluation that is performed systematically at the very end of a specific, pre-determined operating period (such as a week, month, or fiscal year).

questions.label: b
questions.text: "Control is always base-dependent" — Explain.
questions.answer:
Knowledge: Control is always base-dependent because it cannot function in a vacuum; it requires a pre-established plan or standard benchmark to act as its absolute foundation and yardstick.

Realization: The entire purpose of control is to evaluate whether business operations are on the right track. To make this judgment, a manager must have an existing baseline—such as a budget limit, a timeline, or a specific production target. Without this foundational plan, there are no criteria to measure actual performance against, making it impossible to identify deviations or errors. Therefore, planning acts as the necessary base that gives the control process its meaning and direction.

questions.label: c
questions.text: Through which step of control were the production figures of the enterprise in the stem obtained? Explain.
questions.answer:
Knowledge (Para 1): The performance figures of Mr. Shimu's factory were obtained through the second and third steps of the control process, which are "Measuring Actual Performance" (প্রকৃত কার্যফল পরিমাপ) and "Comparing Performance with Standards" (মানদণ্ডের সাথে প্রকৃত কার্যফলের তুলনা).

Contextual Connection (Para 2): The stem indicates that Mr. Shimu set a clear baseline standard of 15,000 pieces of clothing per month. At the end of January and February, his management recorded the real-world factory outputs—13,000 and 13,500 pieces respectively. By actively matching these recorded figures against his original monthly target, he mathematically confirmed shortfalls of 2,000 and 1,500 pieces.

Explanation & Conclusion (Para 3): Performance measurement and comparison are the diagnostic phases of control where managers collect raw field data and place it side-by-side with planned benchmarks. A manager cannot naturally spot a deficit without this active pairing. By executing these steps, Mr. Shimu exposed the negative deviations, which served as the essential administrative warning signs needed before any structural changes could be made on the factory floor.

questions.label: d
questions.text: "The target was achieved precisely due to the adoption of an effective control system" — Evaluate the justification of this statement.
questions.answer:
Knowledge (Para 1): The statement is completely true and justified. The successful turnaround of the factory from March onwards is a direct result of executing the final step of the control loop: "Taking Timely and Corrective Actions" (সংশোধনমূলক ব্যবস্থা গ্রহণ).

Contextual Analysis (Para 2): When faced with production deficits in January and February despite using full capacity, Mr. Shimu did not simply ignore the data or rely on guesswork. He investigated the floor, traced the root causes to a shortage of skilled labor and raw materials, and immediately corrected the system by supplying the missing resources. This active intervention realigned his factory floor with his targets.

Justification & Logical Arguments (Para 3): Implementing this effective control system guaranteed the enterprise's success due to the following strategic reasons:

• Completing the Control Loop: Control is completely useless without its final step—taking corrective action. Measuring performance or finding errors is simply a diagnostic phase; real success comes from intervening to fix the underlying system flaws.

• Eradicating Root Operational Bottlenecks: By directly addressing the shortages of skilled manpower and fabric, Mr. Shimu removed the actual physical constraints that were keeping his assembly lines from hitting their true operating potential.

• Optimizing Resource Efficiency: Supplying qualified personnel reduced material waste and manufacturing delays, allowing the factory to easily convert its raw material inputs into finished, market-ready goods.

• Preventing Compounding Financial Losses: If he had lacked a control system, the 2,000-unit deficit would have continued indefinitely, leading to missed client deadlines, cancelled export orders, and severe financial losses.

Conclusion (Para 4): In conclusion, Mr. Shimu's business turnaround serves as an ideal example of effective management control. Identifying a production shortfall is merely a warning sign; actual corporate success comes from the administrative power to change that trajectory. By turning raw monitoring numbers into rapid field corrections—hiring skilled labor and stabilizing the raw material supply chain—management successfully closed the performance gap, making their ultimate commercial goal fully achievable from March onward.

fullCQData.stem:
The diagram in the image "image_94f148.png" illustrates a stair-step flowchart detailing the chronological stages of the management control process:

1. Setting Standard Benchmarks (আদর্শমান নির্ধারণ)

2. Measuring Actual Performance (কার্যফল পরিমাপ)

3. 'C' (গ)

4. Determining and Evaluating Reasons for Deviation (বিচ্যুতির কারণ নির্ণয় ও মূল্যায়ন)

5. Taking Corrective Actions (সংশোধনমূলক ব্যবস্থা গ্রহণ)

questions.label: a
questions.text: What is a budget?
questions.answer: A budget is the quantitative or numerical expression of a future operational plan formulated for a specific pre-determined period.

questions.label: b
questions.text: "Control activity is base-dependent" — Explain.
questions.answer:
Knowledge: Control is always base-dependent because it cannot function in a vacuum; it requires an existing plan or standard benchmark to act as its absolute foundation and evaluation yardstick.

Realization: The entire purpose of control is to check whether business operations are moving in the right direction. To make this judgment, a manager must have a baseline—such as a budget ceiling, a project timeline, or a production volume target. Without this foundational plan, there are no criteria to measure actual performance against, making it impossible to identify errors. Therefore, planning serves as the indispensable base that gives the control process its meaning and functional direction.

questions.label: c
questions.text: Explain what is represented by the placeholder 'C' (গ) in the stem's diagram.
questions.answer:
Knowledge (Para 1): In the provided diagram from the image "image_94f148.png", the placeholder 'C' (গ) represents the third chronological step of the control process: "Comparing Actual Performance with Standards and Identifying Deviations" (আদর্শমানের সাথে প্রকৃত কার্যফলের তুলনা ও বিচ্যুতি নির্ধারণ).

Theoretical Connection (Para 2): As shown in the step-by-step ladder layout of the flowchart, this function sits directly above measuring performance and directly below analyzing errors. Once a manager has established the target benchmarks (Step 1) and gathered the real-world field metrics (Step 2), they must place these two sets of data side-by-side to cross-examine and mathematically compute the variation between them.

Explanation & Conclusion (Para 3): This step acts as the administrative bridge of the entire control loop. A manager cannot naturally spot a deficit or a surplus by looking at field data alone; it requires active matching against the baseline standard. By identifying whether the resulting deviation is positive, neutral, or negative, this step provides the raw analytical material required for Step 4 (investigating root causes) and Step 5 (taking corrective action).

questions.label: d
questions.text: Analyze the effectiveness and productivity of following the steps shown in the stem's flowchart.
questions.answer:
Knowledge (Para 1): Following the chronological steps shown in the flowchart is highly effective and productive because it provides a systematic, foolproof framework that removes guesswork, ensures workplace discipline, and guarantees the successful execution of organizational plans.

Analytical Evaluation (Para 2): The control process is designed as an interlocking sequence where each step depends completely on the one before it. Skipping any phase breaks the chain. For example, trying to take corrective actions (Step 5) without knowing the root cause (Step 4) or without calculating the exact deviation (Step 3) will lead to arbitrary management decisions that waste company resources.

Justification & Logical Arguments (Para 3): The productivity and institutional benefits of adhering strictly to this structured flowchart include:

• Early Warning and Risk Mitigation: By systematically measuring performance (Step 2) and comparing it with benchmarks (Step 3), managers can spot operational delays or supply deficits early, giving them a window to fix problems before they compound into financial failure.

• Data-Driven Decision Making: Investigating the root causes of performance gaps (Step 4) ensures that managers target the actual bottleneck—such as repairing faulty machines or training slow workers—rather than applying superficial or useless remedies.

• Elimination of Material and Financial Waste: Real-time monitoring keeps production lines running at peak efficiency, minimizing raw material shrinkage, reducing idle labor time, and optimizing total operating costs.

• Boosting Workforce Accountability: When employees know that their outputs are regularly measured against a clear standard, it builds organizational transparency, boosts productivity, and deters workplace negligence or misconduct.

Conclusion (Para 4): In conclusion, the stair-step framework illustrated in the image "image_94f148.png" is the ultimate diagnostic and therapeutic tool for corporate health. Identifying an operational problem is merely a warning sign; actual institutional survival depends on the power to systematically trace and fix that error. By converting raw planning benchmarks into field results through this disciplined sequence, management can eliminate waste, stabilize its supply chain, and ensure that its strategic commercial goals are achieved perfectly and on schedule.

fullCQData.stem: Mr. Kader works in the quality control department of a garments factory. He verifies whether the manufactured garments are matching the required quality standards. Moreover, he constantly monitors production activities so that if any defect is detected, he can resolve it quickly. He considers the standard benchmark as the base for determining deviations.

questions.label: a
questions.text: What is measurement of performance?
questions.answer: Measurement of performance is the specific stage in the control process where actual operational outputs or real-world field results are calculated, recorded, and quantified using specific metrics.

questions.label: b
questions.text: What is meant by standard benchmark (or standard)?
questions.answer:
Knowledge: A standard benchmark (or standard) refers to the pre-determined model, target, or yardstick established during the planning phase, against which the actual performance of an organization is measured and evaluated.

Realization: Standards serve as the corporate baseline for judging operational success or failure. These metrics can be qualitative (such as fabric durability, color accuracy, or customer satisfaction) or quantitative (such as a target of producing 1,000 garments, or a budget limit). Without a clear, predetermined standard, it is impossible to evaluate real-world performance because there is no target baseline to check against. Therefore, setting standard benchmarks acts as the fundamental starting point of the control function.

questions.label: c
questions.text: Which managerial function is Mr. Kader performing in the stem? Explain.
questions.answer:
Knowledge (Para 1): Mr. Kader is performing the core managerial function of "Control" (নিয়ন্ত্রণ), specifically focusing on quality control and real-time operational monitoring.

Contextual Connection (Para 2): The stem indicates that Mr. Kader actively verifies whether the manufactured clothing items match the required quality parameters. He does not wait until the end of the year to check inventory; instead, he constantly monitors the production floor, tracks operational execution, and uses pre-determined standard benchmarks as his baseline to calculate negative variations or manufacturing mistakes.

Explanation & Conclusion (Para 3): Control is the systematic administrative process of measuring actual performance, comparing it with standards, identifying deviations, and taking corrective steps. By physically examining the garments, isolating defects as soon as they appear, and actively applying remedies to fix the machinery or line processes, Mr. Kader executes the entire control loop. His presence ensures that the clothing lines stay aligned with the firm's administrative plans.

questions.label: d
questions.text: "Mr. Kader is minimizing production costs through his activities" — Explain in light of the stem.
questions.answer:
Knowledge (Para 1): The statement is completely accurate and justified. By strictly monitoring the floor and executing real-time quality control, Mr. Kader eliminates manufacturing errors early, which directly reduces material waste, re-work time, and unnecessary operational expenses, thereby minimizing total production costs.

Contextual Analysis (Para 2): In a garments factory, waiting until thousands of pieces are finished before checking their quality is a massive financial risk. If a sewing machine has a hidden alignment defect, it could ruin hundreds of shirts before anyone notices. Mr. Kader’s method of constant, real-time monitoring solves this problem because he intercepts and fixes operational defects the exact moment they happen on the floor.

Justification & Logical Arguments (Para 3): His control activities actively minimize corporate manufacturing costs through the following avenues:

• Reduction of Raw Material Wastage: Catching a stitching or fabric flaw instantly prevents expensive textiles from being thrown into the scrap bin, maximizing fabric utilization rates.

• Elimination of Re-work and Extra Labor Costs: When clothes are made perfectly on the first attempt, the factory does not have to pay laborers extra overtime wages to unpick seams, repair defects, and remake defective units.

• Prevention of Client Penalties and Order Cancellations: In the garment export industry, shipping defective clothing leads to severe financial penalties, expensive air-freight charges, or cancelled shipments from international buyers. Mr. Kader's strict oversight completely protects the firm from these macro financial losses.

• Machinery and Workflow Optimization: Since he resolves defects quickly, machinery breakdowns or assembly bottlenecks are handled immediately, reducing idle worker hours and maximizing daily factory output.

Conclusion (Para 4): In conclusion, Mr. Kader proves that an effective control system is not just an administrative formality but a vital tool for cost optimization. Identifying a manufacturing defect is merely a diagnostic warning; actual financial survival depends on the power to change that trajectory instantly. By converting his analytical quality data into rapid factory floor adjustments, Mr. Kader successfully stops material leakages, increases production efficiency, and successfully drives down the overall manufacturing cost for the enterprise.

fullCQData.stem: Mr. Zaman is a successful businessman. At the beginning of the year, he determines the volume of production and sales for the entire year and performs all managerial activities accordingly. He also identifies the reasons for any deviations, decides on the necessary actions to be taken, and obtains the expected results.

questions.label: a
questions.text: What is a budget?
questions.answer: A budget is the quantitative or numerical expression of a future operational plan formulated for a specific pre-determined period.

questions.label: b
questions.text: Why is control called a terminal (or periodic) task? Explain.
questions.answer:
Knowledge: Control is called a terminal or periodic task because key formal evaluations of performance are systematically carried out at the very end of a specific, pre-determined operating cycle (such as a week, month, or fiscal year).

Realization: Although day-to-day monitoring can happen constantly, the final comparison phase of control cannot take place until an operational period has concluded. Management must wait for the timeline to close so that final actual results can be compiled. Once the period ends, these final figures are cross-examined against the original targets to calculate deviations and wrap up that specific management cycle.

questions.label: c
questions.text: Which function of control does Mr. Zaman perform at the beginning of the year? Describe.
questions.answer:
Knowledge (Para 1): At the beginning of the year, Mr. Zaman performs the first and foundational step of the control process, which is "Setting Standard Benchmarks" (আদর্শমান নির্ধারণ).

Contextual Connection (Para 2): The stem indicates that at the very start of the fiscal cycle, Mr. Zaman fixes the precise volumes for the entire year's production and sales goals. These pre-determined quantitative targets serve as the corporate yardstick that dictates exactly what his business operations are expected to achieve over the next twelve months.

Explanation & Conclusion (Para 3): Control cannot function in a vacuum; it requires a baseline metric to measure success or failure later on. In management, these baseline parameters are established during the standard-setting phase, which borrows directly from the initial planning blueprints. By establishing clear numerical targets for production and sales at the beginning of the year, Mr. Zaman creates the necessary evaluation criteria to check his business's progress down the line.

questions.label: d
questions.text: "Sound control makes the enterprise in the stem successful" — Analyze in light of the stem.
questions.answer:
Knowledge (Para 1): The statement is completely accurate and justified. The continuous success and expected results achieved by Mr. Zaman's enterprise are a direct consequence of executing a "Sound Control System" (সুষ্ঠু নিয়ন্ত্রণ ব্যবস্থা), which effectively bridges the gap between planned targets and real-world results.

Contextual Analysis (Para 2): The stem highlights that Mr. Zaman does not just set targets and leave them on paper. He runs all managerial operations according to those targets, actively evaluates execution, isolates the root causes behind any negative performance gaps, and deploys necessary actions. This complete and disciplined administrative loop is what ensures his firm consistently hits its targets.

Justification & Logical Arguments (Para 3): Implementing a sound control system guarantees organizational success due to the following reasons:

• Completing the Control Loop: Control is completely useless without its final step—taking corrective action. Measuring a shortfall is merely a diagnostic phase; real success comes from intervening to fix the underlying system flaws, which Mr. Zaman does by deciding on "necessary actions."

• Preventing Waste and Inefficiency: By regularly checking actual production and sales figures against his standards, Mr. Zaman can catch operational delays or material waste early, allowing him to fix floor bottlenecks before they turn into major losses.

• Ensuring Plan Execution: Planning sets the direction, but control acts as the continuous guide that keeps the company on that path. A sound control framework ensures that workers, machinery, and capital stay fully aligned with the annual corporate blueprint.

• Optimizing Future Planning Frameworks: Because Mr. Zaman evaluates deviations scientifically, the insights he gains give him a highly realistic view of his firm's true capacity. This factual data allows him to set highly accurate and achievable goals in his next annual planning cycle.

Conclusion (Para 4): In conclusion, Mr. Zaman's business stands as an ideal example of effective administrative control. Many entrepreneurs fail because they stop after discovering an error, leaving the actual problems unaddressed. By turning his analytical performance data into rapid field corrections, Mr. Zaman successfully completed the control loop, minimized operational risks, and turned his administrative blueprints into consistent commercial success.

fullCQData.stem: Mr. Hasnat is the owner of a garments factory. The shirts manufactured in his factory are sold across five showrooms located in two divisional cities. In the month of January, he set a sales target of 50,000 pieces of shirts. On January 16, it was observed that only 15,000 pieces of shirts had been sold. Under these circumstances, Mr. Hasnat became worried. Therefore, along with improving the quality of the clothing, he re-fixed the working hours for the employees and adjusted the sales target.

questions.label: a
questions.text: What is PERT?
questions.answer: PERT (Program Evaluation and Review Technique) is an advanced, network-based statistical control tool used to schedule, organize, and coordinate complex, interrelated tasks within a project to optimize time and resource allocation.

questions.label: b
questions.text: What is meant by budgetary control?
questions.answer:
Knowledge: Budgetary control is a specialized financial control technique where an organization's future plans are prepared in numerical or financial terms (a budget), and actual performance is continuously measured and compared against this budget to correct any financial deviations.

Realization: Under this system, the budget acts as the primary standard benchmark. Management regularly compiles field data and cross-checks actual financial outcomes against the budgeted projections. If expenses run too high or sales run too low, the budget immediately flags these variations, allowing administrators to take swift corrective action to preserve the company's financial health.

questions.label: c
questions.text: With which step of the management process is the determination of the enterprise's deviation on January 16 related? Explain.
questions.answer:
Knowledge (Para 1): Determining the performance deviation on January 16 is directly related to the "Control" (নিয়ন্ত্রণ) function of the management process—specifically the step of "Comparing Actual Performance with Standards and Identifying Deviations" (আদর্শমানের সাথে প্রকৃত কার্যফলের তুলনা ও বিচ্যুতি নির্ধারণ).

Mathematical Calculation (Para 2): To analyze the exact operational shortfall at the mid-month checkpoint, we must look at the pro-rated parameters:

• Total target for January (31 days) = 50,000 shirts

• Mid-month evaluation point = January 16 (approximately 16 days elapsed)

• Pro-rated standard target for 16 days = $50,000 \times \frac{16}{31} \approx 25,806 \text{ shirts}$

• Actual performance achieved by January 16 = 15,000 shirts

• Performance Deviation = Actual Performance $-$ Pro-rated Standard Target

• Deviation = $15,000 - 25,806 = -10,806 \text{ shirts}$ (Deficit)

Explanation & Conclusion (Para 3): Control is the systematic administrative process of ensuring that real-world operations match initial blueprints. A manager cannot naturally spot a deficit without actively pairing field metrics against a baseline target. By reviewing the real-world sales figures (15,000 units) against his pro-rated baseline on January 16, Mr. Hasnat mathematically exposed a severe negative deviation of over 10,800 units, completing the diagnostic comparison phase of control.

questions.label: d
questions.text: Evaluate the logical justification of the corrective measures taken by Mr. Hasnat to solve the organization's problems.
questions.answer:
Knowledge (Para 1): The activities undertaken by Mr. Hasnat—improving garment quality, modifying employee working hours, and revising the target—are highly justified because they represent the final step of the control process: "Taking Corrective Actions" (সংশোধনমূলক ব্যবস্থা গ্রহণ), which is vital to align the business with market realities.

Contextual Analysis (Para 2): Midway through January, the factory faced a massive sales deficit. Instead of ignoring the data, Mr. Hasnat investigated the bottleneck and realized that his initial target might have been overly optimistic or that operational inefficiencies were present. His multidimensional response addresses both structural floor capacity and product marketability.

Justification & Logical Arguments (Para 3): His corrective measures are strategically justified through the following operational phases:

• Enhancing Product Quality: Slow sales often indicate that consumers are rejecting the product line. By actively increasing the manufacturing quality of the shirts, Mr. Hasnat directly boosts brand value and consumer satisfaction, stimulating market demand to lift lagging sales numbers.

• Optimizing Employee Working Hours: To close a production gap or accelerate shipments to the 5 showrooms, managing floor time is critical. Adjusting working hours allows for better capacity utilization, ensuring the factory output matches seasonal demand spikes.

• Revising Targets to Reality: A sound control system recognizes when an initial plan is flawed. If external market forces or internal constraints prevent hitting 50,000 units, lowering the target prevents employee demotivation, reduces stock-holding costs, and prevents financial over-extension.

• Completing the Control Loop: Control is completely useless without its final corrective step. Measuring a shortfall is merely a diagnostic warning; actual corporate recovery comes from the administrative courage to change operational variables on the ground.

Conclusion (Para 4): In conclusion, Mr. Hasnat’s decisive interventions serve as an ideal example of effective administrative control. Many business owners fail because they treat control as a passive mathematical exercise rather than an active field remedy. By turning his analytical mid-month sales data into immediate factory adjustments and strategic revisions, he successfully completed the management loop, protected his showroom supply chain, and steered his garments enterprise back toward profitability.

fullCQData.stem: Fiza Garments received an order to supply 100,000 units of clothing within three months. To execute this order, the company's executive, Mr. Iliyas, began constantly supervising the factory work. He continuously gave the necessary directions to the employees. If any deviation occurred between the plan and the actual work, he took corrective measures. As a result, the target was achieved within the specified time frame.

questions.label: a
questions.text: What is PERT?
questions.answer: PERT (Program Evaluation and Review Technique) is an advanced, network-based statistical control tool used to schedule, organize, and coordinate complex, interrelated tasks within a project to optimize time and resource allocation.

questions.label: b
questions.text: Why is BEP analysis important in control? Explain.
questions.answer:
Knowledge: Break-Even Point (BEP) analysis is highly important in control because it serves as a critical financial benchmark that identifies the exact volume of production or sales where total revenue equals total costs, showing the minimum threshold needed to avoid losses.

Realization: In management control, BEP analysis acts as a precise analytical yardstick. It provides managers with a clear numerical boundary between the company's profit and loss zones. By comparing real-time operational data against this break-even threshold, administrators can instantly determine whether the firm's current sales volume is sufficient to sustain profitability. If performance lags below this point, it sounds an administrative alarm, allowing managers to adjust pricing, reduce fixed/variable costs, or ramp up production speeds to protect the firm from financial distress.

questions.label: c
questions.text: Based on the time frame, what type of plan is the target mentioned in the stem? Explain.
questions.answer:
Knowledge (Para 1): Based on the time frame, the target of manufacturing 100,000 units of clothing within three months is a Short-term Plan (অল্পমেয়াদী পরিকল্পনা).

Contextual Connection (Para 2): In management theory, plans are categorized chronologically based on their operational duration. Any organizational plan or target that is designed to be executed within a period of one year or less is strictly classified as a short-term plan. Since Fiza Garments' operational milestone is capped at a strict three-month export deadline, it falls directly into this category.

Explanation & Conclusion (Para 3): Short-term planning provides the immediate, high-velocity benchmarks needed to handle daily operations and specific commercial orders. For Fiza Garments, this three-month plan established a clear, quantifiable operational target. This brief timeframe allowed Mr. Iliyas to focus his supervisor activities entirely on immediate factory floor capacity, breaking down the 100,000-unit macro goal into manageable daily and weekly output milestones.

questions.label: d
questions.text: "The target was achieved in Fiza Garments precisely due to the establishment of proper control" — Analyze the justification of this statement in light of the stem.
questions.answer:
Knowledge (Para 1): The statement is entirely true and justified. The successful and timely fulfillment of the clothing order at Fiza Garments is a direct result of Mr. Iliyas executing a comprehensive "Control Process" (নিয়ন্ত্রণ প্রক্রিয়া), which effectively kept the factory floor aligned with the initial short-term plan.

Contextual Analysis (Para 2): The stem highlights that Mr. Iliyas did not treat control as a passive evaluation at the end of the three months. Instead, he maintained constant floor supervision, recorded real-time performance, and actively intervened to deploy corrections the exact moment any negative gap or manufacturing mistake appeared. This rigorous, proactive management loop is what guaranteed the project's success.

Justification & Logical Arguments (Para 3): Establishing a proper control system ensured the company's success due to the following strategic reasons:

• Preventing Delay Accumulation: By monitoring operations "constantly," Mr. Iliyas caught processing delays or mechanical breakdowns early. This immediate feedback loop prevented minor daily shortfalls from compounding into a massive, unfixable deficit at the end of the three months.

• Optimizing Human Resource Execution: Continuous direction and field counseling kept the labor force fully focused and motivated. It minimized operational confusion, reduced fabric waste, and ensured that workers maintained the required hourly output speed.

• Completing the Control Loop: Control is completely useless without its final step—taking corrective action. Measuring a shortfall is merely a diagnostic warning; real success comes from intervening to fix the underlying system flaws, which Mr. Iliyas successfully did.

• Safeguarding Corporate Goodwill: For an export-oriented garment firm, missing an order deadline results in severe financial penalties, expensive air-freight charges, or cancelled contracts. Proper control turned a high-pressure deadline into a smooth, structured delivery, protecting the firm's commercial reputation.

Conclusion (Para 4): In conclusion, the case of Fiza Garments stands as an ideal example of effective management principles. Planning merely maps out the destination, but control acts as the continuous steering mechanism that keeps the organization on that path. By turning raw monitoring data into active field corrections, Mr. Iliyas successfully closed all performance gaps, eliminated system inefficiencies, and successfully transformed a challenging short-term target into an outstanding corporate achievement.

fullCQData.stem: Mrs. Sohely Akter is an industrial entrepreneur. She has established an electric bulb manufacturing factory using modern technology. She sets a target to manufacture 24,000 electric bulbs per week. There are three automated machines of equal capacity in the factory for production. The monthly operating time of each machine is fixed at 90 hours. To control the time and quality of production, Mrs. Sohely Akter inspects the production activities twice a day and provides technical and managerial directions as needed.

questions.label: a
questions.text: What is technical skill?
questions.answer: Technical skill is the specialized knowledge, expertise, and proficiency required to perform specific tasks or operate particular machinery, tools, and technological systems effectively within an organization.

questions.label: b
questions.text: Why is the principle of unity of direction important to achieve goals? Explain.
questions.answer:
Knowledge: The principle of unity of direction is vital because it ensures that all activities, efforts, and departments within an organization move toward the same ultimate objective under a single, coordinated plan.

Realization: In management, if different departments or teams work with conflicting plans or separate guidelines, it creates severe administrative overlapping, confusion, and waste of corporate resources. Unity of direction brings harmony by linking every individual effort to the macro organizational blueprint. This alignment eliminates contradictory instructions, speeds up decision-making, and maximizes operational efficiency, making it significantly easier for an enterprise to achieve its long-term strategic goals.

questions.label: c
questions.text: Based on the timeframe in the stem, what is the standard benchmark of production for each machine per hour? Describe.
questions.answer:
Knowledge (Para 1): Based on the production target and the fixed operational hours of the machinery on the factory floor, the standard benchmark of production for each machine is 266.67 electric bulbs per hour.

Mathematical Calculation (Para 2): To find the hourly production standard per machine, we must first convert the weekly target to a monthly baseline (assuming a standard month consists of 4 weeks) and divide it by the total monthly operating hours per machine:

• Weekly target of the factory = 24,000 bulbs

• Monthly target of the factory ($24,000 \times 4 \text{ weeks}$) = 96,000 bulbs

• Total number of identical machines = 3

• Monthly production share per machine ($\frac{96,000 \text{ bulbs}}{3 \text{ machines}}$) = 32,000 bulbs

• Fixed operating runtime per machine per month = 90 hours

• Standard output per machine per hour = $\frac{\text{Monthly Share Per Machine}}{\text{Monthly Operating Hours}}$

• Hourly Standard = $\frac{32,000 \text{ bulbs}}{90 \text{ hours}} = 355.56 \text{ bulbs/hour}$

Alternative standard calculation (assuming 4.33 weeks per month or exact weekly runtime): Let's calculate directly by finding the total monthly output across all machines combined ($3 \text{ machines} \times 90 \text{ hours = 270 total machine hours}$). If we use a standard 4-week conversion for the monthly factory demand ($24,000 \times 4 = 96,000 \text{ bulbs}$):

• Factory Hourly Standard = $\frac{96,000 \text{ bulbs}}{270 \text{ total hours}} = 355.56 \text{ bulbs/hour}$ per machine.

(Note: If the 24,000 target is directly broken down into a standard weekly machine hour framework of 22.5 hours per week ($90 \div 4$), the result remains $\frac{8,000 \text{ bulbs per machine per week}}{22.5 \text{ hours}} = 355.56$ units).

Explanation & Conclusion (Para 3): In management control, establishing an hourly breakdown per machine is an essential part of creating standard benchmarks. The calculation proves that for Mrs. Sohely's factory to hit its macro weekly target, each automated unit must maintain a steady, unyielding operational speed of turning out exactly 355.56 bulbs every single operating hour. This clear baseline allows shop supervisors to evaluate daily output and spot technical lags instantly.

questions.label: d
questions.text: Analyze the logical justification of using the control techniques adopted in the mentioned factory to achieve the targets.
questions.answer:
Knowledge (Para 1): The control techniques adopted by Mrs. Sohely Akter—running close daily field inspections and giving immediate technical-managerial interventions—are highly justified because they represent the dynamic application of "Concurrent Control" (সমসাময়িক নিয়ন্ত্রণ) and real-time oversight.

Contextual Analysis (Para 2): In an automated, high-tech manufacturing plant, waiting until the end of the month to review production figures is a massive financial risk. If a high-speed machine develops a hidden calibration defect, it could ruin thousands of bulbs before anyone notices. Mrs. Sohely’s method of visiting the floor twice a day solves this problem by catching deviations the exact day they manifest.

Justification & Logical Arguments (Para 3): Her proactive control strategy is highly justified due to the following operational benefits:

• Minimizing High-Tech Material Waste: Since the factory runs on automated machinery, quick daily checks ensure that raw material faults or electrical errors are stopped instantly, driving down total production waste.

• Integrating Planning with Execution: By offering "technical and managerial directions" during her inspection rounds, she ensures that human operators and automated assets stay perfectly aligned with the weekly 24,000-unit blueprint.

• Completing the Control Loop: Control is completely useless without its final step—taking corrective action. Merely measuring a shortfall is a diagnostic phase; actual success comes from her active, real-time field interventions.

• Preventing Delay Accumulation: Checking progress twice a day creates an early warning system. This high-frequency feedback loop prevents minor daily shortfalls from compounding into an unfixable weekly deficit.

Conclusion (Para 4): In conclusion, Mrs. Sohely Akter's approach serves as an ideal example of effective management principles. Planning merely maps out the target, but control acts as the continuous steering mechanism that keeps the enterprise on track. By turning her analytical observations into immediate field upgrades and technical corrections, she successfully protects her investment, stabilizes her supply chain, and ensures her factory can hit its ambitious commercial targets on time.

fullCQData.stem: Moon Shoes Ltd., in order to increase the quality of their manufactured products and ensure that officials complete their work on time, outlines the start time, end time, and interrelationships of each task in a chart. This allows them to easily determine which task was not executed on time. On the other hand, Ifad Shoes Ltd. set a target to manufacture 300,000 units of shoes from January 2025 to June 30. However, at the end of June 30, it was observed that 220,000 units of shoes had been produced. The managers of Ifad Shoes Ltd. are worried about this. Upon investigation, they found no material or technical issues.

questions.label: a
questions.text: What is a standing plan?
questions.answer: A standing plan is a long-range, structural organizational blueprint designed to be used repeatedly over an extended period to handle recurring administrative situations, operations, or problems.

questions.label: b
questions.text: Why is flexibility required in planning? Explain.
questions.answer:
Knowledge: Flexibility is required in planning so that the organization can easily adjust, modify, or realign its operational strategies when unexpected changes occur in the volatile external business environment.

Realization: Business environments are inherently unpredictable and subject to sudden shifts in government policies, market demand, competitor strategies, or technological advancements. If a plan is completely rigid and unyielding, the company will face severe financial distress or operational failure when real-world conditions change. Building flexibility into the blueprint allows managers to swiftly pivot, adjust operational targets, and implement backup strategies without disrupting the entire organization, ensuring long-term institutional survival.

questions.label: c
questions.text: Which control technique is used by Moon Shoes Ltd. in the stem? Describe.
questions.answer:
Knowledge (Para 1): The control technique used by Moon Shoes Ltd. in the stem is PERT (Program Evaluation and Review Technique) [or Gantt Chart / Network Analysis].

Contextual Connection (Para 2): The stem indicates that Moon Shoes Ltd. maps out the precise start times, end times, and complex operational "interrelationships" of each corporate activity in a structured diagram. By visualizing how these tasks connect, management can track chronological workflows in real time and immediately pinpoint exactly which specific department or task is lagging behind the master schedule.

Explanation & Conclusion (Para 3): PERT is an advanced, network-based administrative control tool used to coordinate and monitor complex, interrelated tasks within a project. It establishes a visual flow of events, highlighting the critical path and dependencies between milestones. By utilizing this time-centric scheduling matrix, Moon Shoes Ltd. successfully eliminates floor blind spots, preserves operational transparency, and ensures that individual officials can be held strictly accountable to time and quality guidelines.

questions.label: d
questions.text: Calculate the deviation for Ifad Shoes Ltd. in the stem and evaluate the logical justification of the corrective measures needed to eliminate this deviation.
questions.answer:
Knowledge (Para 1): Ifad Shoes Ltd. experienced a severe negative performance deviation (ঋণাত্মক বিচ্যুতি) of 80,000 units of shoes at the end of their six-month operating cycle.

Mathematical Calculation (Para 2): To determine the exact operational shortfall, we place the pre-determined planning targets side-by-side with the recorded field metrics:

• Established standard target (Jan to June 30) = 300,000 units

• Actual performance achieved by June 30 = 220,000 units

• Performance Deviation = Actual Performance $-$ Standard Target

• Deviation = $220,000 - 300,000 = -80,000 \text{ units}$ (Deficit)

Contextual Analysis & Justification (Para 3): Since management investigated the floor and confirmed that there are absolutely no material shortages or technical engineering issues holding back production, the root cause behind this massive 80,000-unit deficit must lie entirely within the human element. Therefore, the logical corrective actions must shift away from hardware upgrades and focus completely on labor productivity and organizational behavior:

• Auditing Employee Efficiency & Motivation: Management must analyze whether the shortfall stems from low worker morale, poor factory floor supervision, or inadequate assembly line training.

• Re-evaluating Workforce Accountability: Introducing labor-focused corrective steps—such as setting individual worker performance quotas, offering performance-based financial incentives, or adjusting shifts—is highly justified to correct employee slowness.

• Upgrading Floor Leadership: Since raw materials and machinery are working perfectly, strengthening direct factory supervision will stop workers from wasting time and ensure they maintain the necessary hourly output speed.

Conclusion (Para 4): In conclusion, calculating a negative deviation of 80,000 units serves as a severe diagnostic warning sign for Ifad Shoes Ltd. Measuring an error is useless unless management completes the control loop by deploying targeted interventions. By recognizing that their technical systems are flawless, the company's managers are perfectly justified in focusing their corrective actions entirely on restructuring labor management, boosting floor discipline, and realigning human productivity to successfully hit their next macro manufacturing target.

fullCQData.stem: "Good Food" is a manufacturing enterprise. In the year 2024, 100,000 units of products were manufactured in the enterprise. For each unit of product manufactured, the raw material requirement is 1,000 Tk., and the wage per unit is 200 Tk. The enterprise pays building rent of 250,000 Tk. and officers' salaries of 500,000 Tk. The selling price of each product is 2,000 Tk. The annual electricity bill of the enterprise to be paid is 50,000 Tk.

questions.label: a
questions.text: What is budgetary control?
questions.answer: Budgetary control is a specialized financial control technique where an organization's future plans are prepared in numerical or financial terms (a budget), and actual performance is continuously measured and compared against this budget to correct any financial deviations.

questions.label: b
questions.text: Why is control called a "terminal (or periodic) task"? Explain.
questions.answer:
Knowledge: Control is called a terminal or periodic task because key formal evaluations of performance are systematically carried out at the very end of a specific, pre-determined operating cycle (such as a week, month, or fiscal year).

Realization: Although day-to-day monitoring can happen constantly, the final comparison phase of control cannot take place until an operational period has concluded. Management must wait for the timeline to close so that final actual results can be compiled. Once the period ends, these final figures are cross-examined against the original targets to calculate deviations and wrap up that specific management cycle.

questions.label: c
questions.text: Determine the Break-Even Point (BEP) for the "Good Food" company.
questions.answer:
Knowledge (Para 1): The Break-Even Point (BEP) in units for "Good Food" company is 1,000 units, and the Break-Even Point in value is 2,000,000 Tk.

Mathematical Calculation (Para 2): To find the BEP, we must first categorize the data into Total Fixed Costs (FC) and Variable Cost per Unit (VC):

• Selling Price per Unit ($P$) = 2,000 Tk.

1. Variable Cost per Unit ($VC$):

• Raw Material Cost per Unit = 1,000 Tk.

• Wage per Unit = 200 Tk.

• Total Variable Cost per Unit ($VC$) = $1,000 + 200 = 1,200 \text{ Tk.}$

2. Total Fixed Costs ($FC$):

• Building Rent = 250,000 Tk.

• Officers' Salaries = 500,000 Tk.

• Annual Electricity Bill = 50,000 Tk.

• Total Fixed Costs ($FC$) = $250,000 + 500,000 + 50,000 = 800,000 \text{ Tk.}$

3. Break-Even Point (BEP) Formula and Calculation:

$$\text{BEP (in units)} = \frac{\text{Total Fixed Costs (FC)}}{\text{Selling Price per Unit (P)} - \text{Variable Cost per Unit (VC)}}$$

$$\text{BEP (in units)} = \frac{800,000}{2,000 - 1,200} = \frac{800,000}{800} = 1,000 \text{ units}$$

$$\text{BEP (in sales value)} = 1,000 \text{ units} \times 2,000 \text{ Tk./unit} = 2,000,000 \text{ Tk.}$$

Explanation & Conclusion (Para 3): In management control, the break-even point serves as a critical quantitative baseline. The calculation proves that "Good Food" company must manufacture and sell exactly 1,000 units of its product (generating 2,000,000 Tk. in revenue) to reach a state of zero profit and zero loss. Selling anything below this threshold will result in structural financial losses for the factory.

questions.label: d
questions.text: Conduct a comparative analysis of the two types of costs present in the stem.
questions.answer:
Knowledge (Para 1): The two types of expenses present in the stem are Fixed Costs (স্থায়ী ব্যয়) and Variable Costs (পরিবর্তনশীল ব্যয়), which maintain entirely opposite behavioral relationships with the company's total production volume.

Contextual Differentiation (Para 2): In the stem, the total fixed cost sums up to 800,000 Tk. (composed of rent, salaries, and utility bills), while the variable cost per unit stands at 1,200 Tk. (composed of raw materials and direct factory labor). The core distinction lies in how these values respond when "Good Food" changes its manufacturing scale.

Justification & Comparative Analysis (Para 3): A deep comparative evaluation of these two cost structures reveals the following insights:

• Relationship with Production Volume: Fixed costs remain completely stagnant at 800,000 Tk. regardless of whether the factory produces 0 units or its full capacity of 100,000 units. Conversely, variable costs increase in direct proportion to volume; producing 100,000 units requires a massive aggregate variable layout of 120,000,000 Tk. ($100,000 \times 1,200$).

• Per-Unit Cost Behavior: While the variable cost remains locked at a flat 1,200 Tk. per individual item, the fixed cost per unit drops drastically as volume ramps up. At full 100,000-unit capacity, the fixed cost burden is a mere 8 Tk. per product ($\frac{800,000}{100,000}$), maximizing the profit margin per unit.

• Managerial Control Flexibility: Variable costs are highly controllable in the short term; if sales drop, management can instantly reduce raw material purchases. Fixed costs, however, are structural and rigid; the company must pay the 250,000 Tk. building rent even if factory operations are completely halted.

Conclusion (Para 4): In conclusion, understanding the comparative interplay between fixed and variable costs is an absolute prerequisite for effective financial control. While variable costs represent the direct, operational expense of creation, fixed costs represent the organizational infrastructure. By aggressively maximizing production up to 100,000 units, "Good Food" successfully dilutes its fixed overhead per unit down to just 8 Tk., allowing the firm to secure a high-profit safety margin well above its 1,000-unit break-even threshold.

fullCQData.stem:
The diagram in the image "image_94dae6.png" illustrates a sequential flowchart detailing the steps of a core management process:

1. Measurement of Performance (কার্যফল পরিমাপ)

2. Determination of Deviation (বিচ্যুতি নিরূপণ)

3. Evaluation of Deviation (বিচ্যুতি মূল্যায়ন)

4. Taking Corrective Measures (সংশোধনমূলক ব্যবস্থা গ্রহণ)

questions.label: a
questions.text: What is auditing?
questions.answer: Auditing is the systematic, independent, and official examination of an organization's financial records, accounts, and operational activities to verify accuracy, compliance, and reliability.

questions.label: b
questions.text: Is there any step after control? If yes, what is it? Explain.
questions.answer:
Knowledge: Yes, there is an indirect next step after the control process, which is "Planning" (পরিকল্পনা) for the subsequent operating cycle.

Realization: Management is an ongoing, cyclical process. Control is the final phase of a current cycle, but it never acts as a permanent dead end. The performance data, deviations found, and corrective feedback recorded during the control stage serve as the foundational parameters and lessons upon which the next cycle's plans are designed. Therefore, once control ends, it immediately links back to initiate a fresh planning phase, keeping the organizational cycle moving continuously.

questions.label: c
questions.text: Which function of the management process is consistent with the flowchart above? Explain.
questions.answer:
Knowledge (Para 1): The flowchart illustrated in the upper diagram from the image "image_94dae6.png" is completely consistent with the "Control" (নিয়ন্ত্রণ) function of the management process.

Theoretical Connection (Para 2): Control is the final, corrective stage of administrative management. The chart outlines the exact, universally recognized chronological milestones of a control system: recording real-world field results (Measurement of Performance), calculating the positive or negative numerical gap between output and plans (Determination of Deviation), analyzing the root causes of that gap (Evaluation of Deviation), and deploying administrative fixes (Taking Corrective Measures).

Explanation & Conclusion (Para 3): Management functions run in a systematic sequence beginning with planning and ending with control. The presence of steps like tracing performance variations and executing corrections proves that the diagram is a visual model of corporate quality and execution tracking. Therefore, the chart represents the operational loop of the control process, which ensures that all organizational activities align with executive blueprints.

questions.label: d
questions.text: "The flowchart above is incomplete and therefore ineffective" — Evaluate the justification of this statement.
questions.answer:
Knowledge (Para 1): I completely agree with the statement. The flowchart in the image "image_94dae6.png" represents an incomplete and structurally ineffective control process because it completely omits the first and most critical foundational step, which is "Setting Standard Benchmarks" (আদর্শমান নির্ধারণ).

Analytical Evaluation (Para 2): Looking closely at the sequence in the diagram, it attempts to initiate control directly with performance measurement. In management theory, control is an interlocking chain where each step depends entirely on the one preceding it. If you remove the initial baseline standard, the subsequent steps shown in the chart—measurement, deviation tracking, and evaluation—lose their reference point and become impossible to execute in the real world.

Justification & Logical Arguments (Para 3): The omission of standard-setting renders the illustrated process incomplete and ineffective due to the following reasons:

• Lack of an Evaluation Yardstick: Control fundamentally means checking for operational mistakes. If an organization does not establish a clear standard first (such as a budget ceiling, a time deadline, or a production volume target), managers have no baseline criteria to judge actual performance against.

• Impossibility of Determining Deviations: Deviation is calculated by subtracting the standard target from actual performance. If there is no pre-determined standard, step 2 (Determination of Deviation) becomes a mathematical impossibility because there is no baseline figure to compare field metrics against.

• Blind Corrective Measures: Without a predefined benchmark, taking corrective action (Step 4) turns into blind, arbitrary guesswork. Managers cannot fairly hold workers accountable or optimize factory workflows unless there is an established standard to guide their administrative corrections.

• Disconnection from Planning: The missing standard-setting step is the exact bridge where planning and control meet. A standard is simply the target borrowed from the operational plan; omitting it completely isolates control from the company's strategic goals.

Conclusion (Para 4): In conclusion, the flowchart presented in the stem is structurally flawed because it attempts to build an evaluative system without a foundation. Finding an operational error or measuring field success is impossible if you do not know what success looks like in the first place. By failing to include the standard-setting phase before performance measurement, the entire sequence becomes dysfunctional. To make this chart effective, it must begin with a clear, data-driven standard benchmark, which secures the essential reference point that makes performance tracking, deviation analysis, and corrective actions fully functional, accurate, and successful.

fullCQData.stem: A textile mill sets a target to manufacture 8,000 meters of cloth daily. However, at the end of the month, it was observed that the average daily production was 6,000 meters. Upon deep observation, the manager found that frequent power outages and a shortage of skilled labor were the primary reasons for this shortfall.

questions.label: a
questions.text: What is budgetary control?
questions.answer: Budgetary control is a specialized financial control technique where an organization's future plans are prepared in numerical or financial terms (a budget), and actual performance is continuously measured and compared against this budget to correct any financial deviations.

questions.label: b
questions.text: "Budget is a key yardstick of control" — Explain.
questions.answer:
Knowledge: Budget is considered a key yardstick of control because it provides the concrete, quantitative, and numerical baseline data against which actual operational outputs are compared.

Realization: The primary function of control is to evaluate whether business operations are on the right track. To make this judgment, a manager must have an exact, pre-determined benchmark. When a plan is expressed numerically as a budget (such as target sales volumes, cost limits, or production units), it removes all ambiguity. It serves as a precise administrative scale that highlights any structural profit leaks or production drops instantly, allowing management to execute the control function accurately.

questions.label: c
questions.text: Describe the control process illustrated in the stem.
questions.answer:
Knowledge (Para 1): The control process illustrated in the stem involves the sequential execution of Setting Standard Benchmarks (আদর্শমান নির্ধারণ), Measuring Performance (কার্যফল পরিমাপ), and Analyzing Deviations (বিচ্যুতির কারণ নির্ণয় ও মূল্যায়ন).

Contextual Application (Para 2): The management of the textile mill completed three core milestones of the control loop:

• Step 1: Setting Standards: The mill established a daily standard baseline of 8,000 meters of cloth.

• Step 2: Measuring & Comparing Performance: At the end of the month, they recorded an actual daily average of 6,000 meters, thereby revealing a clear negative performance deviation of 2,000 meters per day ($6,000 - 8,000 = -2,000 \text{ meters}$).

• Step 3: Analyzing Deviations: Instead of merely acknowledging the shortfall, the manager conducted a deep operational audit and traced the root causes to external infrastructure issues (frequent power outages) and human resource constraints (shortage of skilled workers).

Explanation & Conclusion (Para 3): The control process is a systematic diagnostic cycle used by administrators to track organizational health. By moving from a macro planning target to real-world statistical measurement, and finally to root-cause identification, the manager successfully completed the diagnostic phase of the control framework. This clear data prepares the enterprise for the final, therapeutic stage of management action.

questions.label: d
questions.text: "Control is meaningless without taking corrective measures for deviations" — Evaluate the statement in light of the stem.
questions.answer:
Knowledge (Para 1): The statement is completely true and justified. Merely identifying a production deficit of 2,000 meters and its causes is a passive exercise; control only gains economic and administrative value when management moves to the final step of "Taking Corrective Actions" (সংশোধনমূলক ব্যবস্থা গ্রহণ).

Contextual Analysis (Para 2): In the stem, the manager successfully diagnosed why the textile mill was lagging behind its daily capacity. However, if the manager stops at this stage and fails to deploy active field remedies, the daily 2,000-meter gap will continue indefinitely, leading to unfulfilled client contracts, massive financial losses, and ultimate business failure.

Justification & Logical Arguments (Para 3): Failing to take corrective action renders the entire control loop meaningless due to the following reasons:

• Leaving System Flaws Unresolved: Knowing that power outages and unskilled labor are ruining production speeds does not fix the assembly lines. Action must be taken to remove these physical constraints.

• Waste of Administrative Resources: The time, money, and labor spent tracking daily outputs and running diagnostic audits are completely wasted if the factory floor is allowed to remain in an inefficient state.

• Ramping up Operational Capacity: To eliminate the 2,000-meter deficit, the manager must actively implement corrective field solutions—such as installing a heavy-duty backup industrial generator to counter power outages and arranging intensive training programs or hiring skilled weavers to solve the labor shortage.

• Fulfilling Planning Commitments: Planning sets the destination, but corrective control is the actual steering mechanism that brings a straying company back onto the path of profitability.

Conclusion (Para 4): In conclusion, diagnosing an operational error is merely an administrative warning sign; actual corporate survival depends on the power to change that bad trajectory. By turning raw analytical data into rapid field corrections—such as securing backup power systems and upgrading labor expertise—management successfully closes the performance gap, eliminates waste, and ensures the textile mill can realistically achieve its desired commercial goals.

fullCQData.stem: Mr. Hasnat is the owner of a garments factory. The shirts manufactured in his factory are sold across five showrooms located in two divisional cities. In the month of January, he set a sales target of 50,000 pieces of shirts. However, by January 16, it was observed that only 15,000 pieces of shirts had been sold. Under these circumstances, he became worried. Therefore, along with improving the quality of the clothing, he is going to adjust the working hours for the employees and re-fix the sales target.

questions.label: a
questions.text: What is the full form of PERT?
questions.answer: The full form of PERT is Program Evaluation and Review Technique.

questions.label: b
questions.text: What is meant by budgetary control?
questions.answer:
Knowledge: Budgetary control is a specialized financial control technique where an organization's future plans are prepared in numerical or financial terms (a budget), and actual performance is continuously measured and compared against this budget to correct any financial deviations.

Realization: Under this system, the budget acts as the primary standard benchmark. Management regularly compiles field data and cross-checks actual financial outcomes against the budgeted projections. If expenses run too high or sales run too low, the budget immediately flags these variations, allowing administrators to take swift corrective action to preserve the company's financial health.

questions.label: c
questions.text: With which step of the management process is the determination of the enterprise's deviation on January 16 related? Explain.
questions.answer:
Knowledge (Para 1): Determining the performance deviation on January 16 is directly related to the "Control" (নিয়ন্ত্রণ) function of the management process—specifically the step of "Comparing Actual Performance with Standards and Identifying Deviations" (আদর্শমানের সাথে প্রকৃত কার্যফলের তুলনা ও বিচ্যুতি নির্ধারণ).

Mathematical Calculation (Para 2): To analyze the exact operational shortfall at the mid-month checkpoint, we look at the pro-rated parameters:

• Total target for January (31 days) = 50,000 shirts

• Mid-month evaluation point = January 16 (16 days elapsed)

• Pro-rated standard target for 16 days = $50,000 \times \frac{16}{31} \approx 25,806 \text{ shirts}$

• Actual performance achieved by January 16 = 15,000 shirts

• Performance Deviation = Actual Performance $-$ Pro-rated Standard Target

• Deviation = $15,000 - 25,806 = -10,806 \text{ shirts}$ (Deficit)

Explanation & Conclusion (Para 3): Control is the systematic administrative process of ensuring that real-world operations match initial blueprints. A manager cannot naturally spot a deficit without actively pairing field metrics against a baseline target. By reviewing the real-world sales figures (15,000 units) against his pro-rated baseline on January 16, Mr. Hasnat mathematically exposed a severe negative deviation of over 10,800 units, completing the diagnostic comparison phase of control.

questions.label: d
questions.text: Evaluate the logical justification of the corrective measures adopted by Mr. Hasnat to solve the organization's problems.
questions.answer:
Knowledge (Para 1): The activities undertaken by Mr. Hasnat—improving garment quality, modifying employee schedules, and adjusting the sales target—are highly justified because they represent the final step of the control process: "Taking Corrective Actions" (সংশোধনমূলক ব্যবস্থা গ্রহণ), which is vital to align the business with market realities.

Contextual Analysis (Para 2): Midway through January, the factory faced a massive sales deficit. Instead of ignoring the data, Mr. Hasnat investigated the bottleneck and realized that his initial target might have been overly optimistic or that operational inefficiencies were present. His multidimensional response addresses both structural floor capacity and product marketability.

Justification & Logical Arguments (Para 3): His corrective measures are strategically justified through the following operational phases:

• Enhancing Product Quality: Slow sales often indicate that consumers are rejecting the product line. By actively increasing the manufacturing quality of the shirts, Mr. Hasnat directly boosts brand value and consumer satisfaction, stimulating market demand to lift lagging sales numbers.

• Optimizing Employee Schedules: To close a production gap or accelerate shipments to the showrooms, managing floor time is critical. Adjusting working schedules allows for better capacity utilization, ensuring the factory output matches seasonal demand spikes.

• Revising Targets to Reality: A sound control system recognizes when an initial plan is flawed. If external market forces or internal constraints prevent hitting 50,000 units, lowering the target prevents employee demotivation, reduces stock-holding costs, and prevents financial over-extension.

• Completing the Control Loop: Control is completely useless without its final corrective step. Measuring a shortfall is merely a diagnostic warning; actual corporate recovery comes from the administrative courage to change operational variables on the ground.

Conclusion (Para 4): In conclusion, Mr. Hasnat’s decisive interventions serve as an ideal example of effective administrative control. Many business owners fail because they treat control as a passive mathematical exercise rather than an active field remedy. By turning his analytical mid-month sales data into immediate factory adjustments and strategic revisions, he successfully completed the management loop, protected his showroom supply chain, and steered his garments enterprise back toward profitability.

fullCQData.stem: Mr. Russel Mollick is the owner of "Mollick Brothers." Every year, at the beginning of the year, he determines the potential production and sales targets. In addition, based on other potential expenses, he prepares an income statement and compares it with the actual expenses. If any deviation occurs, he investigates the reasons for the deviation and takes corrective measures. Due to this step of his, the expenses in the organization have decreased significantly.

questions.label: a
questions.text: What is a standard benchmark (or standard)?
questions.answer: A standard benchmark (or standard) refers to the pre-determined model, target, or quantitative/qualitative yardstick established during the planning phase, against which actual operational performance is measured and evaluated.

questions.label: b
questions.text: Explain why control is called a terminal (or periodic) task.
questions.answer:
Knowledge: Control is called a terminal or periodic task because key formal evaluations of performance are systematically carried out at the very end of a specific, pre-determined operating cycle (such as a week, month, or fiscal year).

Realization: Although day-to-day monitoring can happen constantly, the final comparison phase of control cannot take place until an operational period has concluded. Management must wait for the timeline to close so that final actual results can be compiled. Once the period ends, these final figures are cross-examined against the original targets to calculate deviations and wrap up that specific management cycle.

questions.label: c
questions.text: Which type of control method did Mr. Mollick use in his organization? Explain.
questions.answer:
Knowledge (Para 1): Mr. Russel Mollick uses "Budgetary Control" (বাজেটীয় নিয়ন্ত্রণ) as the primary control method in his enterprise.

Contextual Connection (Para 2): The stem indicates that at the beginning of the year, Mr. Mollick forecasts potential production, sales volumes, and upcoming operational costs to compile a predictive income statement. In management theory, expressing a future operational plan entirely in financial or numerical terms creates a budget. By later placing real-world operational costs side-by-side with this document, he actively uses the budget as an administrative baseline to track institutional variance.
