Explanation & Conclusion (Para 3): Budgetary control is a specialized technique where the budget acts as the primary standard benchmark. Mr. Mollick doesn't just treat the numbers as static guesses; he uses them to isolate cost inflation, investigate floor leaks, and execute corrective adjustments. This disciplined tracking is exactly what allows "Mollick Brothers" to systematically reduce its raw expenditure and maintain strict financial health.

questions.label: d
questions.text: An organization can never reach its desired goals without a proper control system. Analyze your opinion in light of the stem.
questions.answer:
Knowledge (Para 1): I completely agree with the statement. A proper control system is the ultimate safeguarding mechanism in management; without it, organizational plans remain vulnerable to floor inefficiencies, and hitting targets becomes impossible.

Contextual Analysis (Para 2): The case of "Mollick Brothers" serves as a perfect example of this principle. Mr. Mollick’s business didn't lower its costs simply by setting ambitious production and sales goals. The financial savings came entirely from the active control phase—identifying numerical gaps between budgeted estimates and real-world costs, uncovering the root causes of those errors, and deploying targeted fixes.

Justification & Logical Arguments (Para 3): A proper control system is an absolute prerequisite for an enterprise to reach its desired goals due to the following reasons:

• Bridging Planning with Execution: Planning merely maps out the target destination, but control acts as the continuous steering mechanism that keeps the company on that path. Without it, deviations go unnoticed, and plans fall apart.

• Preventing Resource and Capital Leakage: As demonstrated in the stem, regularly comparing real expenses with standard targets allows management to catch material waste, worker slowness, or luxury spending early, which drives down total operating costs.

• Completing the Management Loop: Control is completely useless without its final step—taking corrective action. Measuring a shortfall is merely a diagnostic phase; real success comes from intervening to fix system flaws, ensuring future operational safety.

• Optimizing Future Performance Baselines: Because Mr. Mollick scientifically tracks variations, the data he gathers gives him a highly realistic view of his firm's true capacity. This factual data allows him to set highly accurate and achievable goals in his next annual planning cycle.

Conclusion (Para 4): In conclusion, an organization without control is like a ship without a rudder—it has a direction but no way to stay on course. Identifying a cost overrun or a production shortfall is merely an administrative warning sign; actual corporate survival depends on the power to change that trajectory. By turning raw monitoring data into immediate adjustments, a proper control system eliminates waste, stabilizes workflows, and successfully transforms an entrepreneur's abstract business targets into predictable, profitable realities.

fullCQData.stem: Jonaki Ltd. is a manufacturing enterprise. In the year 2018, 100,000 units of products were manufactured in the enterprise. For each unit of product manufactured, the raw material requirement is 800 Tk., and the wage per unit is 400 Tk. The enterprise pays building rent of 200,000 Tk. and officers' annual salaries of 550,000 Tk. The selling price of each product is 2,000 Tk. The annual electricity bill of the enterprise to be paid is 50,000 Tk.

questions.label: a
questions.text: What is teleconferencing?
questions.answer: Teleconferencing is an interactive electronic communication method that allows multiple individuals in geographically separated locations to connect, converse, and hold live meetings simultaneously via telecommunication links and audio technology.

questions.label: b
questions.text: What is meant by the principle of balance in coordination? Explain.
questions.answer:
Knowledge: The principle of balance in coordination means ensuring that the work schedule, speed, capacity, and resource allocation among various interrelated departments or individuals within an organization are perfectly matched and proportioned.

Realization: In management, if one department (such as Production) works at an extremely high speed while another linked department (such as Sales or Procurement) lags far behind, it creates severe structural bottlenecks, idle inventory, or operational chaos. The principle of balance establishes equilibrium across the enterprise. It ensures that no single division is overburdened or underutilized, allowing the entire organizational workflow to move smoothly, systematically, and efficiently toward its unified target.

questions.label: c
questions.text: Determine the Break-Even Point (BEP) production for Jonaki Ltd.
questions.answer:
Knowledge (Para 1): The Break-Even Point (BEP) in units for Jonaki Ltd. is 1,000 units, and the Break-Even Point in value is 2,000,000 Tk.

Mathematical Calculation (Para 2): To find the BEP, we must first categorize the given financial figures into Total Fixed Costs (FC) and Variable Cost per Unit (VC):

• Selling Price per Unit ($P$) = 2,000 Tk.

1. Variable Cost per Unit ($VC$):

• Raw Material Cost per Unit = 800 Tk.

• Wage per Unit = 400 Tk.

• Total Variable Cost per Unit ($VC$) = $800 + 400 = 1,200 \text{ Tk.}$

2. Total Fixed Costs ($FC$):

• Building Rent = 200,000 Tk.

• Officers' Annual Salaries = 550,000 Tk.

• Annual Electricity Bill = 50,000 Tk.

• Total Fixed Costs ($FC$) = $200,000 + 550,000 + 50,000 = 800,000 \text{ Tk.}$

3. Break-Even Point (BEP) Formula and Calculation:

$$\text{BEP (in units)} = \frac{\text{Total Fixed Costs (FC)}}{\text{Selling Price per Unit (P)} - \text{Variable Cost per Unit (VC)}}$$

$$\text{BEP (in units)} = \frac{800,000}{2,000 - 1,200} = \frac{800,000}{800} = 1,000 \text{ units}$$

$$\text{BEP (in sales value)} = 1,000 \text{ units} \times 2,000 \text{ Tk./unit} = 2,000,000 \text{ Tk.}$$

Explanation & Conclusion (Para 3): In management control, the break-even point serves as a critical quantitative baseline. The calculation proves that Jonaki Ltd. must manufacture and sell exactly 1,000 units of its product (generating 2,000,000 Tk. in revenue) to reach a state of equilibrium where total revenue perfectly matches total costs. Selling anything below this 1,000-unit threshold will result in structural financial losses for the enterprise.

questions.label: d
questions.text: Conduct a comparative analysis of the two types of costs present in the stem.
questions.answer:
Knowledge (Para 1): The two types of expenditures present in the stem are Fixed Costs (স্থায়ী ব্যয়) and Variable Costs (পরিবর্তনশীল ব্যয়), which maintain entirely opposite behavioral relationships with the company's total production volume.

Contextual Differentiation (Para 2): In the stem, the total fixed cost sums up to 800,000 Tk. (composed of rent, annual salaries, and utility bills), while the variable cost per unit stands at 1,200 Tk. (composed of raw materials and direct factory labor). The core distinction lies in how these values react when Jonaki Ltd. alters its manufacturing scale.

Justification & Comparative Analysis (Para 3): A deep comparative evaluation of these two cost structures reveals the following insights:

• Relationship with Production Volume: Fixed costs remain completely stagnant at 800,000 Tk. regardless of whether the factory produces 0 units or its full capacity of 100,000 units. Conversely, variable costs increase in direct proportion to volume; producing the maximum 100,000 units requires a massive aggregate variable layout of 120,000,000 Tk. ($100,000 \times 1,200$).

• Per-Unit Cost Behavior: While the variable cost remains locked at a flat 1,200 Tk. per individual item, the fixed cost per unit drops drastically as volume ramps up. At full 100,000-unit capacity, the fixed cost burden is a mere 8 Tk. per product ($\frac{800,000}{100,000}$), which drastically expands the profit margin per unit.

• Managerial Control Flexibility: Variable costs are highly controllable in the short term; if sales drop, management can instantly reduce raw material purchases. Fixed costs, however, are structural and rigid; the company must pay the 200,000 Tk. building rent even if factory operations are completely halted.

Conclusion (Para 4): In conclusion, understanding the comparative interplay between fixed and variable costs is an absolute prerequisite for effective financial control. While variable costs represent the direct, operational expense of creation, fixed costs represent the organizational infrastructure. By aggressively maximizing production up to 100,000 units, Jonaki Ltd. successfully dilutes its fixed overhead per unit down to just 8 Tk., allowing the firm to secure a high-profit safety margin well above its 1,000-unit break-even threshold.

fullCQData.stem: Dishari Denims exports denim goods worth 25 crore Tk. per month, totaling 100 crore Tk. annually. In the year 2030, at the end of 6 months, their export volume stands at only 30 crore Tk. As the export target was not achieved, Bishal Denims decided to supervise the managerial processes.

questions.label: a
questions.text: What is the full form of PERT?
questions.answer: The full form of PERT is Program Evaluation and Review Technique.

questions.label: b
questions.text: Why is control called a terminal (or periodic) task? Explain.
questions.answer:
Knowledge: Control is called a terminal or periodic task because key formal evaluations of performance are systematically carried out at the very end of a specific, pre-determined operating cycle (such as a week, month, or fiscal year).

Realization: Although day-to-day monitoring can happen constantly, the final comparison phase of control cannot take place until an operational period has concluded. Management must wait for the timeline to close so that final actual results can be compiled. Once the period ends, these final figures are cross-examined against the original targets to calculate deviations and wrap up that specific management cycle.

questions.label: c
questions.text: At which step of the control process did Dishari Denims realize that the target was not achieved? Explain.
questions.answer:
Knowledge (Para 1): Dishari Denims realized that its target was not achieved at the step of "Comparing Actual Performance with Standards and Identifying Deviations" (আদর্শমানের সাথে প্রকৃত কার্যফলের তুলনা ও বিচ্যুতি নির্ধারণ) in the control process.

Mathematical Calculation (Para 2): To understand the exact deficit at the 6-month checkpoint, we must cross-examine the planned standards against recorded performance:

• Baseline standard target per month = 25 crore Tk.

• Expected standard target for 6 months ($25 \text{ crore} \times 6$) = 150 crore Tk.

(Note: Even if calculated against a pro-rated annual target of 100 crore Tk., the expected standard for 6 months is 50 crore Tk.)

• Actual export performance achieved in 6 months = 30 crore Tk.

• Performance Deviation = Actual Performance $-$ Standard Target

• Deviation = $30 \text{ crore} - 50 \text{ crore} = -20 \text{ crore Tk.}$ (or $-120 \text{ crore Tk.}$ based on monthly run-rate standard).

Explanation & Conclusion (Para 3): Control is the administrative phase where field outputs are mathematically matched against planned benchmarks. A negative gap remains invisible until this comparative analysis is executed. By placing their real 6-month export data (30 crore Tk.) side-by-side with their established baseline standard, Dishari Denims explicitly uncovered a severe negative deviation, signaling an immediate operational failure to management.

questions.label: d
questions.text: Determine the actions to be taken by Bishal Denims to achieve the export target in the remaining period.
questions.answer:
Knowledge (Para 1): To retrieve the massive export deficit in the remaining 6 months of the year, Bishal Denims must execute the final, therapeutic stage of control: "Taking Corrective Actions" (সংশোধনমূলক ব্যবস্থা গ্রহণ), backed by deep process auditing.

Contextual Analysis (Para 2): The firm has a massive target gap to bridge in the final half of 2030. Simply wanting higher sales will not change field realities. Since the stem mentions that Bishal Denims decided to "supervise the managerial processes," the corrective remedies must stem from a thorough diagnostic audit of the factory floor and the supply chain.

Justification & Logical Actions (Para 3): To hit the macro annual goals in the remaining time, management must execute the following corrective steps:

• Identify Root Bottlenecks: Management must immediately audit why the output dropped—whether due to labor strikes, fabric shortages, mechanical failures in production machinery, or a drop in international buyer orders.

• Optimize Assembly Floor Efficiency: If the shortage is labor-related, the firm must introduce extra overtime production shifts, optimize clothing assembly lines, or deploy advanced garment machinery to increase the monthly manufacturing speed.

• Strengthen Quality Control (QC): Export orders are frequently rejected or delayed due to minor quality errors. Upgrading direct supervisor tracking on the floor prevents clothing defects, eliminating time-wasting re-work and ensuring smooth delivery speeds.

• Re-negotiate with International Buyers: If the bottleneck stems from supply chain logistics or shipping delays, marketing executives must coordinate directly with overseas buyers to expedite freight or secure extensions without incurring financial penalties.

Conclusion (Para 4): In conclusion, detecting a commercial deficit is merely an administrative warning; enterprise survival relies completely on the power to alter that trajectory. Bishal Denims cannot rely on outdated routines if they wish to recover the lost 20 to 120 crore Tk. deficit. By transforming raw monitoring data into aggressive floor adjustments, upgrading resource allocation, and optimizing factory workflows during the remaining months, management can eliminate waste, stabilize its supply chain, and ensure the company's strategic export goals are met before the fiscal year ends.
